title: Inventory captive portal ownership and cutover contract
description: Decide and record the exact M5 migration contract before code moves: current portal tables, API procedures, write paths, UniFi dependencies, hostnames, TLS, CNA constraints, and rollback shape. This avoids lifting accidental Control Center coupling into the product boundary.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Current portal source tables, read paths, write paths, cron jobs, UniFi dependencies, and hostname/TLS behavior are inventoried in the ticket notes.
- [ ] Target product API/database/network contract is recorded, including same-origin/internal API preference and app.cp.worldwidewebb.co acceptance target.
- [ ] Rejected alternatives are recorded, especially preserving Control Center API coupling, public Cloudflare routing, and generic LAN exposure.
- [ ] Human-review checkpoints for data movement, LAN TLS, UniFi redirect, firewall/walled-garden, final snapshot, and production guest onboarding are explicitly listed.
- [ ] No implementation code is changed by this decision ticket.
notes:
# Decision: Captive Portal M5 migration contract

## Non-technical assumptions
- Guest onboarding behavior must not regress: email code flow, returning-device states, session-expired state, original-url redirect, and internet authorization remain the user-visible contract.
- The production target hostname for the product migration is `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`, but Apple Captive Network Assistant compatibility still requires the raw-IP HTTP path to stay supported unless later evidence proves otherwise.
- Calum review is required before any live data movement, LAN TLS cutover, UniFi mutation, final production guest onboarding cutover, or destructive cleanup.

## Current inventory

### Product/runtime ownership
- Frontend: `apps/captive-portal` Vite/React app, package `@cc/captive-portal`, static nginx image `control-center-captive-portal`.
- Frontend API client: `apps/captive-portal/src/lib/trpc.ts` uses same-origin `httpBatchLink` at `/api/trpc`, typed via `@cc/api/trpc` / Control Center `AppRouter`.
- nginx boundary: `apps/captive-portal/nginx.conf` serves the SPA on `:80` and `:443`; `_portal_locations.conf` proxies only `/api/trpc/portal.*` batches and returns 404 for all other `/api/*`.
- Current upstream: portal nginx rewrites `/api/trpc/portal.*` to `/trpc/portal.*` and proxies to `http://api.control-center.svc.cluster.local:4201`, so production is still coupled to the Control Center API.
- Deployed workload: `infra/src/services.ts` declares `captive-portal` in the Control Center namespace with LAN LoadBalancer ports `80` and `443`, mounting `captive-portal-tls`.

### Current Control Center API/data ownership
- Router: `apps/api/src/trpc/routers/portal.ts` registers `portal.sendCode`, `portal.verifyCode`, `portal.checkPassword`, `portal.authorize`, `portal.status`, and `portal.resetAttempts` on the global Control Center tRPC router.
- Service: `apps/api/src/services/portal-service.ts` owns flow semantics, typed `PortalError`s, server-side cooldown/lockout, DB-authoritative authorization windows, and UniFi reconcile-on-status.
- Repo adapter: `apps/api/src/services/portal-repo.ts` is the SQL boundary over Control Center Drizzle/Postgres.
- Email: `portal.ts` chooses Resend when `RESEND_API_KEY` and `RESEND_FROM` exist; production refuses to boot with the mock sender.
- UniFi: `portal.ts` injects the Control Center UniFi integration. Guests never call UniFi directly.
- Secrets/config currently involved: `WIFI_PASSWORD`, `RESEND_API_KEY`, `RESEND_FROM`, `UNIFI_*` via Control Center API config, plus `POSTGRES_PASSWORD` for Control Center Postgres.

### Current source tables and write paths
- `portal_guest`: `gst_` id, `name`, `email`, `created_at_utc`. Created by `sendCode`; newest row by email is reused for resend/cooldown lookup.
- `portal_code`: `otp_` id, `guest_id`, plaintext 6-digit `code`, `expires_at_utc`, `consumed`, `created_at_utc`; indexed by `(guest_id, consumed)`. Created by `sendCode`; previous codes consumed by resend; consumed by successful or expired verify.
- `portal_attempt`: `att_` id, `mac`, `kind` (`code` or `password`), `wrong_count`, `window_started_at_utc`, `locked_until_utc`; unique `(mac, kind)`. Written by wrong code/password attempts, cleared on success/back/resend.
- `portal_authorization`: `auth_` id, `mac`, `guest_id`, `granted_at_utc`, `expires_at_utc`; unique `mac`. Upserted by `authorize`; read by `status` for `fresh` / `active` / `expired`.
- Controller side effect: `authorize` calls UniFi `authorize-guest` for 43,200 minutes after the DB upsert; `status` best-effort heals a missing active UniFi grant without downgrading the DB row.

### Current scheduled/data jobs
- `infra/src/crons.ts` declares `portal-data-purge` at `0 2 * * *`, running the API image command `bun purge.js` with `POSTGRES_HOST` set to the Control Center CNPG RW service.
- `infra/src/secrets-map.ts` maps `portal-data-purge` to `Control Center Postgres/password` only.
- Purge semantics live in `apps/api/src/services/portal-purge-service.ts`: cleanup is Control Center DB-local today.

### Current hostname/TLS/LAN behavior
- Current nice browser hostname: `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Current certificate: `infra/src/certmanager.ts` issues `captive-portal-tls` for `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` via cert-manager + Cloudflare DNS-01.
- Current nginx `server_name`: `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` on TLS listener.
- Current LAN edge: `scripts/portal-443-forward.sh` raw-TCP forwards mini LAN `192.168.0.147:443` to the k8s `captive-portal` LoadBalancer `:443`; OrbStack exposes `:80` but not `:443` reliably.
- Current public edge: `infra/cloudflare/src/routes.ts` intentionally excludes captive portal from tunnel ingress and proxied CNAMEs. This no-tunnel invariant must remain true.
- Current Apple CNA constraint: `docs/captive-portal/runbook.md` records that the iOS captive sheet works over raw-IP HTTP `http://192.168.0.147/...` and fails to render the same app through the real hostname, even with valid cert and gzip off. Gzip must stay off.

### Current UniFi dependencies
- Controller: UniFi Cloud Gateway Fiber at `https://192.168.0.1`, API key in 1Password Homelab item `UniFi/local_api_key`, private controller API is read-write.
- Guest SSID: `www-guest`; UniFi network name: `guest`; VLAN 20; open WLAN, portal-gated, not WPA-gated.
- Split DNS: `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co -> 192.168.0.147` currently adopted in `infra/unifi`.
- Firewall: `www-guest -> portal (.147) 80/443` allow rule at index 22000, with network/client isolation otherwise.
- Guest access: current runbook readback records external portal state for `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co`; provider ownership is partial/adopt-heavy, so any change to `guest_access`, walled-garden, or redirect fields needs readback and review.
- Walled garden / restrictions: pre-auth allows `192.168.0.147`; post-auth restrictions keep RFC1918 ranges blocked.

## Target M5 contract

### Product boundary
- Captive Portal becomes product-owned under the platform product boundary, with product-owned frontend, backend/API, Postgres database, backup path, runtime config, tests, and deploy ownership.
- Control Center API must no longer be the production portal API once cutover completes. It may temporarily contain legacy code/tables during the rollback window only.
- Portal API should expose only the captive flow: `sendCode`, `verifyCode`, `checkPassword` if still retained by product flow, `authorize`, `status`, and `resetAttempts`. Non-portal procedures and mixed tRPC batches remain rejected.
- Frontend should keep same-origin/internal API preference: browser calls `/api/trpc`, nginx or product web routes to the product API internally. Guests must never reach dashboard procedures, Home Assistant, or UniFi directly.

### Product database and migration
- Captive Portal gets its own CNPG Postgres cluster and product-owned secrets/connection string.
- Portal rows are migrated with stable existing IDs (`gst_`, `otp_`, `att_`, `auth_`), timestamps, FK relationships, consumed/expired code semantics, attempt lockout state, and active/expired authorization semantics intact.
- Migration tooling must produce a custom-format dump, SQL gzip, row-count report, semantic validation report, scratch restore/import result, and rollback artifact before any final switch.
- Old Control Center portal tables remain untouched until after production cutover, first post-cutover backup, and restore validation are complete.

### Network/TLS/runtime
- Acceptance target hostname: `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` with valid LAN-served TLS via DNS-01 certificate coverage.
- Captive Portal remains LAN-only: no Cloudflare tunnel ingress or proxied CNAME for the portal product.
- The raw-IP HTTP path for Apple CNA remains supported unless a later validated change replaces it. SPA assets and `/api/trpc/portal.*` must work on that path.
- Gzip remains off for captive portal nginx.
- LAN edge/forwarding must deliberately point to the product-owned service while preserving `:80` and `:443` behavior.

### UniFi
- Split DNS, guest redirect, pre-auth allowances, firewall pinhole, and post-auth restrictions must be updated deliberately for `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` while preserving raw-IP CNA behavior.
- UniFi writes must be preceded by a readback baseline and a human approval checkpoint; provider-managed fields should be preferred only where preview/readback proves exact ownership.
- Rollback must restore previous `guest_access`, DNS, firewall, and walled-garden values from the baseline.

## Rejected alternatives
- Preserve Control Center API coupling and only move the static frontend: rejected because product isolation would be false, portal data would remain in Control Center Postgres, and guest/API boundary risk would continue to depend on dashboard router state.
- Keep portal tables in Control Center Postgres and treat them as shared platform state: rejected because M5 requires product-owned storage, backups, restore validation, and rollback independent from Control Center.
- Public Cloudflare tunnel route for Captive Portal: rejected because the portal is a captive-network/LAN product, not public internet surface. It would also weaken the no-tunnel invariant and conflict with guest-network/raw-IP behavior.
- Generic LAN exposure platform primitive now: rejected because North Star explicitly keeps Captive Portal as a special captive-network exposure until another real product needs generic LAN web exposure.
- Blind UniFi API writes for `guest_access`/walled-garden: rejected because prior probing showed ambiguous fields on UniFi OS 10.4.57. Use console/readback or provider ownership only after preview proves fidelity.
- Dropping legacy Control Center tables during cutover: rejected until post-cutover backup and restore validation prove the product DB path.

## Human-review checkpoints
- Before creating or mutating production Captive Portal CNPG resources.
- Before any production portal data export, import, write freeze, restore, final snapshot, or semantic validation action against live data.
- Before switching production runtime from Control Center API/Postgres to product API/Postgres.
- Before LAN TLS/hostname cutover from `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` to `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Before UniFi redirect, split DNS, firewall, guest_access, pre-auth allowance, or walled-garden mutations.
- Before real production guest onboarding validation on `www-guest`.
- Before destructive legacy cleanup: dropping old portal tables, removing old API routes, retiring old hostname/DNS, or removing rollback routes.

## Follow-up work
- Implementation remains in the existing M5 child tickets: CC-jtp0.5.3, CC-jtp0.5.4, CC-jtp0.5.5, CC-jtp0.5.6, CC-jtp0.5.7, CC-jtp0.5.8, CC-jtp0.5.9, CC-jtp0.5.10, and CC-jtp0.5.11.
- No implementation code was changed for this decision ticket.
close_reason: Recorded M5 captive portal inventory, target contract, rejected alternatives, human-review checkpoints, and no-code decision evidence in ticket notes.

The old route cannot be retired until the shipped kiosk build targets the new private URL and Access headers still work.

title: Implement private-web Cloudflare Access policy contract

acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Access declarations derive from privateWeb exposures.
- [ ] The default-deny wildcard Access app for *.worldwidewebb.co is modeled as a deliberate protected policy, not an accidental side effect.
- [ ] app.cc supports kiosk service-token access, Storybook/Drizzle-style tooling supports email OTP, and future app.amp private access is representable.
- [ ] Hook/CI service-token behavior is preserved or explicitly retired by the CI/deploy migration, never broken by human-only SSO.
- [ ] Secrets for Access service-token client secrets stay in 1Password/GitHub or Pulumi secret config, never plaintext.

- [ ] Verification: Access unit tests pass, gitleaks/no-personal-email guard remains clean, and Cloudflare preview shows only reviewed Access changes.

notes: Human review checkpoint: Cloudflare Access policy changes, especially wildcard block floor and dashboard private gate, require approval before apply.
close_reason: Implemented privateWeb-derived Cloudflare Access model with wildcard default-deny, app.cc kiosk service-token policy, hooks CI service-token policy, email-OTP tooling policies, secret/config-key-only principals, tests, guards, preview, and full gates.

title: Define product exposure contract for networking/TLS

acceptance_criteria:

title: Derive Cloudflare tunnel and DNS routes from product exposures

title: M9: Rename And Polish
description: Rename and polish after production state is stable: rename repository to world-wide-webb, update docs, badges, CI labels, image naming, Pulumi project naming, and references, and remove old repo/control-center identity from load-bearing paths.
design:
## Goal
Rename the repository and remaining platform-wide identity from the legacy `control-center` repo shape to `world-wide-webb` after M5, M6, M7, and M8 are production-stable, without breaking product deploys, images, Pulumi state, badges, docs, backups, or operator runbooks.

## Scope
- Rename the GitHub repository identity to `world-wide-webb` with an explicit human-approved cutover plan.
- Replace load-bearing repository references in README badges, GitHub Actions labels, workflow comments, scripts, operational docs, agent instructions, and generated badge URLs.
- Move package/workspace naming from repo-era `@repo/*` and bridge-only `@cc/*` names to platform/product-aware names that still keep product runtime boundaries clear.
- Rename GHCR image naming and CI digest collection from `control-center-*` to the target product/platform image names, keeping deployment rollback possible.
- Rename Pulumi project names, config namespaces, resource type tokens, stack references, and descriptions only through a previewed, state-safe migration path.
- Add an identity-drift audit/guard so old load-bearing repo identity does not re-enter after the rename.
- Remove old paths/resources/aliases only after the renamed repo, images, Pulumi stacks, and products are proven stable.

## Out Of Scope
- No product feature work.
- No product database migration, backup redesign, or route policy redesign beyond references created by the rename.
- No hostname cutover for product app/API surfaces unless a stale repo identity is embedded in a route label or docs-only reference.
- No secret rotation unless a secret name must change for the rename and has a dedicated human checkpoint.
- No broad refactor of product folders, platform primitives, or CI topology beyond polishing identity names.

## Current State
- The GitHub repository and README badges point at `0x63616c/control-center`.
- The root package is named `control-center`; internal workspaces use `@repo/*` plus a browser-safe `@cc/api` bridge.
- CI builds and reads GHCR images named `ghcr.io/0x63616c/control-center-{web,api,worker,media-worker,storybook,drizzle,captive-portal,map-provision}`.
- The main Pulumi project is `control-center`; separate projects are `control-center-cloudflare` and `control-center-unifi`; the deployment config namespace is `ccinfra:`.
- Kubernetes namespace, CNPG service/database names, backup filenames, and historical docs still contain `control-center` or `control_center`; some may be product-owned and intentionally retained for the Control Center product, while repo-level identity should be renamed.
- `README.md`, `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `infra/Pulumi*.yaml`, `infra/src/*`, package manifests, scripts, and operational docs contain identity references that must be classified before changing.

## Target State
- The repository is named `world-wide-webb`, and README badges, GitHub links, workflow labels, clone instructions, and operational docs reference the new repo.
- Product-specific `control-center` names remain only where they mean the Control Center product, namespace, database, service, or product route.
- Platform/repo-level names use `world-wide-webb` or a documented short form consistently.
- Package names clearly separate platform packages from product packages and do not imply Control Center owns shared runtime code.
- CI publishes and deploys the target image names, and the deploy job reads digests from those names with a rollback path to the previous `control-center-*` images.
- Pulumi projects/config namespaces/resource tokens are renamed only after `pulumi preview` proves no unintended replacements of live resources.
- An audit command fails if old repo identity remains in load-bearing paths outside an explicit allowlist.

## Work Breakdown
1. Inventory repo identity references and add the identity-drift audit contract.
2. Rename GitHub repository references, README badges, workflow display labels, and local/bootstrap docs.
3. Rename workspace/package scopes to platform/product-aware names.
4. Migrate CI image naming and GHCR digest/deploy plumbing.
5. Rename Pulumi project/config/resource identity safely with preview and state handling.
6. Polish operational docs, agent instructions, scripts, and generated references.
7. Cut over and verify production after the rename.
8. Destructively clean up old aliases, image tags, and obsolete repo/resource paths after stability.

## Dependencies
- M9 implementation depends on M5, M6, M7, and M8 production stability before any risky cutover.
- The inventory/audit issue blocks all rename implementation issues.
- Package naming should land before image naming so CI names reflect the final product/platform model.
- Image naming and Pulumi naming must both land before production cutover verification.
- Docs polish follows the concrete rename work so docs describe the actual final state.
- Destructive cleanup depends on successful production cutover and explicit human approval.

## TDD Structure
- Identity audit: first add a failing scan that detects the current repo-level `control-center` references, then narrow it with an allowlist for product-owned Control Center references.
- Repository/docs rename: first update or add badge/link validation that fails on `github.com/0x63616c/control-center` and old raw badge URLs.
- Package rename: first run typecheck/knip against a branch with expected new package names missing to confirm import/package resolution fails, then fix imports/manifests.
- Image naming: first add/update CI-image-name tests or dry-run checks that expect new GHCR names and fail against the old `control-center-*` tags.
- Pulumi rename: first run `pulumi preview --diff` and capture any planned replacements; implementation must fail/stop if replacements are not the intended aliases/state moves.
- Docs polish: existing docs/links checks and identity audit protect behavior.
- Cutover/cleanup: verification is operational, no code changes without preceding gates.

## Verification
- `bun install --frozen-lockfile` if package names or lockfile entries change.
- `bun run test`, `bun run typecheck`, `bunx biome check .`, and `bun run knip` from a normal checkout.
- `bun run badges` and README badge URL inspection after repo rename.
- GitHub Actions CI green on the renamed repo.
- GHCR manifests resolve for every target image name and digest collection prints all expected services.
- `pulumi preview --stack prod --diff --cwd infra` plus equivalent cloudflare/unifi previews before any Pulumi state rename.
- `pulumi up --stack prod` only after human approval for image/Pulumi cutover.
- `kubectl get deploy,cronjob,svc -n <product namespaces>` confirms all products are deployed.
- Product smoke checks: Control Center dashboard true 1366x1024 screenshot, Captive Portal LAN check, Text Your Ex app/API health, AMP private app health.
- Identity audit passes with only documented product-owned allowlist entries.

## Rollback / Cutover
- Repository rename: GitHub redirects are expected, but rollback is renaming the repo back and restoring README/badge URLs if CI/webhooks fail.
- Package rename: rollback by reverting package manifest/import changes before deployment.
- Image rename: keep old `control-center-*` images published until the new image names have deployed and rolled at least once; rollback by repointing digest collection/Pulumi image bases to old image names.
- Pulumi rename: use aliases/state moves where needed; do not apply if preview shows unintended replacements. Rollback by restoring prior Pulumi project/config namespace and reselecting the previous stack before applying.
- Destructive cleanup waits until all products verify on the new identity and Calum explicitly approves deletion of old image tags/resources/aliases.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Approve the GitHub repository rename from `control-center` to `world-wide-webb` before it happens.
- Approve final package scope convention before mass import/package rename.
- Approve GHCR image namespace/name cutover before CI starts publishing/deploying only new names.
- Approve Pulumi project/config namespace/state migration after reviewing previews and intended state moves.
- Approve any secret, backup path, database name, Kubernetes namespace, or Cloudflare resource rename that could affect production state.
- Approve destructive cleanup of old repo paths, old GHCR images, old Pulumi stacks, stale DNS/routes, and compatibility aliases.

## Docs To Update
- `README.md`, badge links, clone/setup snippets, and deploy-path overview.
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `CLAUDE.md` repo/platform identity references.
- `docs/platform/*.html` if the final naming convention differs from the current migration docs.
- `docs/deployment-design.md`, `docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md`, and recovery cheatsheets for image/Pulumi/project rename behavior.
- CI workflow comments and generated Pulumi preview labels.
- Scripts and runbooks that mention `control-center` as a repo rather than the Control Center product.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] All products remain deployed.
- [ ] CI remains green.
- [ ] Old repo/control-center identity is removed from load-bearing paths.
notes: Implementation dependency note: depends on M5/M6/M7/M8 production stability. Kept as notes, not blocker links, so the scope ticket remains ready for planning.
close_reason: All M9 ACs complete: repo renamed to world-wide-webb, Pulumi namespace ccinfra->wwwinfra, GHCR images renamed to www-cc-*/www-amp-app, identity audit clean, CI green on renamed identity (dispatch 27602098675), prod running www-cc-* images. 9.8+9.9 closed.

title: M8: AMP Product
description: Build and deploy AMP as a minimal real product: use private Cloudflare Access exposure, prove the standard product path for a new app, and include app route, optional API route, CI/deploy, logs, health, and database/backups if declared.
design:
## Goal
Ship AMP (Application Management Plane) as a minimal real internal product that proves the new platform product path: product folder/workspace, static app shell, image build, k8s workload, private Cloudflare Access route at app.amp.worldwidewebb.co, health/log verification, and no unnecessary API or database until durable state is actually needed.

## Scope
- Create the AMP v0 product shell under the platform product layout, using the standard web app conventions that M4 establishes.
- Build AMP as a small Vite/React static app served by nginx, with a health endpoint or static health artifact suitable for k8s/route smoke checks.
- Add AMP to the product-aware CI/image/deploy path so an AMP-only change builds/deploys only AMP plus shared platform dependencies.
- Deploy AMP to the homelab k3s target through platform service primitives, not one-off k8s YAML.
- Expose only app.amp.worldwidewebb.co as privateWeb through Cloudflare Access.
- Verify production logs, health, TLS, Access behavior, and rollback.
- Explicitly do not declare an AMP API or database in v0 unless a child issue introduces a concrete stateful feature.

## Out Of Scope
- Full AMP feature set, operational dashboards, database management UI, project creation flows, or platform admin mutations.
- Public AMP routes.
- api.amp.worldwidewebb.co, unless a later AMP feature proves an external API is needed.
- AMP database/CNPG cluster, unless a later feature needs durable state. If declared later, platform-managed backup/restore validation is mandatory.
- Repo rename to world-wide-webb.
- Reworking Control Center, Captive Portal, or Text Your Ex product migrations beyond consuming the platform seams they already provide.

## Current State
- There is no products/ directory in the checkout yet. Root workspaces currently cover apps/*, packages/*, infra, infra/unifi, and infra/cloudflare.
- Static web product patterns exist in apps/web and apps/captive-portal: Bun workspace, Vite/React, tsc build, nginx runtime image, CI buildx arm64 image jobs, and Pulumi digest pinning.
- infra/src/services.ts is Control Center-specific, hardcoding GHCR image names as control-center-<service>, the control-center namespace, WorkloadSpec objects, and service route ports.
- infra/src/cluster.ts exports APP_NAMESPACE = control-center, so per-product namespace derivation belongs to the earlier platform foundation/product-folder milestones.
- infra/cloudflare currently adopts live routes only: dashboard, storybook, drizzle, hooks, portainer, and a stray hooks-test CNAME. There is no AMP route.
- infra/cloudflare Access currently adopts only storybook and drizzle email-OTP apps. The wildcard default-deny and dashboard service-token plan are not deployed yet. AMP private access must use the M3 privateWeb contract rather than adding a bespoke Access rule.
- .github/workflows/ci.yml path filters and digest loops do not include AMP. Build/deploy loops currently know web, api, worker, media-worker, storybook, drizzle, captive-portal, and map-provision.

## Target State
- products/amp exists as a first-class product workspace, with package name and scripts following the product convention established by M4.
- AMP v0 renders a minimal management-plane shell with no fake data, no placeholder operational claims, true-black base background for the app page, and dark-mode friendly styling.
- AMP has a container image, nginx health/smoke surface, and structured deploy metadata through platform primitives.
- AMP runs in the amp product namespace on homelab k3s with only the resources and secrets it declares. v0 declares no secrets, no external API, and no database.
- app.amp.worldwidewebb.co is routed through Cloudflare tunnel and protected by Cloudflare Access privateWeb policy. No api.amp route exists.
- CI/deploy includes AMP path filters, image publishing, digest collection, and Pulumi config wiring, without rebuilding/deploying unrelated products for AMP-only changes.
- Production verification proves Access gating, TLS, app health, pod/service health, logs, and rollback.

## Work Breakdown
1. CC child: Add AMP product workspace shell, creates the minimal app and red-first smoke/component tests.
2. CC child: Containerize AMP static app with health surface, adds nginx/Dockerfile and hermetic image/config tests.
3. CC child: Register AMP in platform product/deploy definitions, declares AMP identity, namespace, workload intent, and no API/database in v0.
4. CC child: Add AMP CI image and deploy wiring, adds path filter, build job, digest collection, and deploy config flow.
5. CC child: Expose AMP through private Cloudflare Access, adds app.amp route and Access policy through M3 networking primitives.
6. CC child: Verify AMP production cutover, performs human-reviewed deploy, smoke checks, logs/health checks, and rollback proof.
7. CC child: Update platform and operator docs for AMP, records how to operate, verify, and later extend AMP.

## Dependencies
- Implementation depends on M1 Platform Foundation for app identity/service/exposure/database/secret primitives.
- Implementation depends on M4 Product Folders And CI Split for products/amp workspace conventions and product-aware workflows.
- AMP private route/access work depends on M3 Networking And TLS Contract.
- Child dependency order: workspace shell -> container/health -> product deploy registration -> CI/deploy wiring -> private route/access -> production verification -> docs closeout.
- The route/access and production deploy issues carry explicit human-review checkpoints before applying the Cloudflare/Pulumi changes.

## TDD Structure
- AMP shell: write a failing component/smoke test first that expects the management-plane heading, build metadata/empty-state copy, and no fake operational metrics.
- Container/health: write a failing hermetic test for nginx health response and SPA fallback/asset behavior before Docker/nginx changes.
- Product/deploy registration: write failing Pulumi/render tests proving AMP namespace/workload names, no secrets, no database, no api.amp route, and derived labels before implementation.
- CI/deploy: write/update workflow validation checks or script tests first so AMP is present in path filters, build job, digest collection, and deploy dependency lists.
- Private route/access: update route/access tests first to expect app.amp.worldwidewebb.co privateWeb and reject public/api.amp by default.
- Production verification/docs: use existing gates and live smoke checks rather than code tests.

## Verification
- Local/focused: bun run --filter <amp-package> test, typecheck, build once the package exists.
- Infra/cloudflare: bun run --filter @repo/infra test/typecheck and bun run --filter @repo/cloudflare test/typecheck, or successor product-scoped commands after M1/M4.
- Root gates before shipping implementation: bun run test, bun run typecheck, bunx biome check ., bun run knip.
- CI: push branch/main through .github/workflows/ci.yml and confirm AMP image builds only for AMP/shared changes, and deploy reads the AMP digest key.
- Production smoke: kubectl get deploy/service in namespace amp, kubectl logs deploy/<amp-app>, HTTP health check at the cluster service, Cloudflare/TLS check for app.amp.worldwidewebb.co, Access unauthenticated denial, authenticated allow, and no api.amp DNS/route unless explicitly introduced.

## Rollback / Cutover
- AMP is additive. Rollback starts by disabling/removing the app.amp Cloudflare route/Access app, then scale AMP workload to 0 or remove the AMP service declaration.
- Keep the image and product folder in git if rollback is only production-facing. Revert code only if the product path itself is wrong.
- Since v0 declares no database, rollback has no data migration step. If a future child declares a database, it must add the standard backup/restore rollback contract before production deploy.
- Do not remove or alter existing dashboard/storybook/drizzle/hook routes as part of AMP cutover.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Before creating or changing Cloudflare route/Access policy for app.amp.worldwidewebb.co.
- Before the first production Pulumi apply that creates the AMP namespace/workload/route.
- Before declaring an AMP database or external api.amp route in any future issue.
- Before any cleanup that deletes AMP production resources after a rollback.

## Docs To Update
- docs/platform/PRODUCTS.html: AMP v0 surface and explicit no API/database decision.
- docs/platform/NETWORKING.html: app.amp privateWeb verification and api.amp remains absent by default.
- docs/platform/PLATFORM_PRIMITIVES.html: any new product/app declaration example if AMP proves a new primitive shape.
- docs/platform/DATA_AND_BACKUPS.html: only if AMP declares a database later, otherwise record that v0 is stateless.
- docs/deployment-design.md and docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md if CI/deploy, route, or operational recovery paths change.
- CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md and AGENTS.md if new product workspace conventions become agent-facing rules.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] AMP has an app route.
- [ ] AMP has an optional API route if declared.
- [ ] AMP has CI/deploy.
- [ ] AMP has logs.
- [ ] AMP has health checks.
- [ ] AMP has platform-owned database/backups if it declares a database.
notes: Implementation dependency note: depends on M1/M3/M4. Kept as notes, not blocker links, so the scope ticket remains ready for planning.

title: M7: Control Center Product
description: Make Control Center product-owned without breaking the wall panel: preserve home integrations through product boundaries, cut over to the product CNPG cluster with export/restore rehearsal/row-count validation/final snapshot/rollback, keep workers running, and update the hosted iOS kiosk URL to the new private route.
design:
## Goal
Make Control Center a first-class `control-center` product under the world-wide-webb platform without breaking the wall panel, home automation workers, media worker, iOS kiosk, production data, or backups.

## Scope
- Move Control Center runtime ownership behind the product boundary established by M4: web, api, worker, media-worker, Storybook, iOS kiosk shell, Docker/build metadata, and local dev entrypoints.
- Preserve existing runtime behavior while changing ownership and declarations: same wall panel, same `/trpc` same-origin model, same Home Assistant/UniFi/Sonos/Spotify/NAS integrations, same fixed 1366x1024 target.
- Declare Control Center through product/platform primitives: product namespace, service declarations, secret access, private app exposure, database, backup, and CI/deploy wiring.
- Cut over to the Control Center product CNPG cluster using the data cutover contract: inventory, pre-migration dumps, restore rehearsal, row-count and semantic validation, write freeze, final snapshot, deploy, post-cutover backup restore validation, rollback retention.
- Move the hosted iOS kiosk production URL from `https://dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` to `https://app.cc.worldwidewebb.co`, preserving CF Access service-token header injection and watchdog reload behavior.
- Verify production after cutover: api health, worker loops, media-worker, cron jobs, private route, iPad/TestFlight, and true 1366x1024 wall-panel screenshot.

## Out Of Scope
- Building or redesigning Control Center product features, tiles, or layout beyond migration-required compatibility.
- Migrating Captive Portal, Text Your Ex, or AMP work, except for respecting dependencies and not rebuilding/deploying Control Center on unrelated product changes.
- Creating an external `api.cc.worldwidewebb.co` route unless a concrete external client need is discovered and approved.
- Retiring `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` before TestFlight uptake and wall-panel verification pass.
- Deleting old database/PVC/dump artifacts during M7.
- Cloud k3s or non-homelab deployment targets.

## Current State
- Code is still repo/app shaped: `apps/web`, `apps/api`, `apps/worker`, `apps/media-worker`, `apps/web/ios`, `packages/api`, and `packages/logger`.
- No `products/control-center` folder exists yet.
- `packages/api` is a browser-safe type bridge: it re-exports only `AppRouter` from `@repo/api/trpc`.
- `packages/logger` is platform-shared and should stay shared.
- Infra currently has Control Center-specific declarations in `infra/src/services.ts`, `infra/src/cnpg.ts`, `infra/src/crons.ts`, `infra/src/secrets-map.ts`, and `infra/src/component.ts`.
- Current app workload set includes `api`, `worker`, `media-worker`, `web`, `storybook`, `captive-portal`, `drizzle`, `cloudflared`, plus `map-provision` image use.
- Current CNPG cluster uses database `control_center`, owner `postgres`, secret `cc-postgres-auth`, service `control-center-rw`, and storage `5Gi`.
- Current backup CronJob is hardcoded as `pg-backup`, writes `control_center-YYYYMMDD.sql.gz` under `backups/postgres` on the NAS export.
- CI builds GHCR images named `control-center-*`, path filters are rooted in `apps/*`, `packages/*`, and `infra/**`, and deploy must keep using `ccinfra:imageDigests.<svc>`.
- The iOS kiosk default URL is `https://dashboard.worldwidewebb.co`; native Swift already injects CF Access service-token headers when built with secrets and watchdog probes/reloads use those headers.
- `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` is the target private route. `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` must remain live until iOS and wall-panel cutover verification pass.

## Target State
- Control Center is product-owned under `products/control-center` or has explicit compatibility wrappers with documented removal path.
- Platform/shared code remains outside the product: logger stays shared, product primitives own infra wiring, and the web's API type bridge remains browser-safe.
- Control Center product services are declared through platform primitives with derived names/labels/namespaces/secrets/backups where available, not duplicated low-level Kubernetes string soup.
- `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` is the private Cloudflare Access-gated Control Center app route. Same-origin `/trpc` reaches the internal api. No external `api.cc` route exists by default.
- Control Center runs on a product CNPG cluster with platform-managed nightly backup at `/volume1/Homelab/backups/world-wide-webb/control-center/postgres/` or the platform-derived equivalent.
- Data is restored and validated before cutover, then final-snapshotted and validated again during cutover, with the old DB/PVC/dumps retained for rollback.
- Worker and media-worker loops continue to run with real integrations and no new persistent failures.
- TestFlight build points at `https://app.cc.worldwidewebb.co`; installed iPad kiosk loads the new private route and recovers via watchdog.
- Wall panel verifies at a true 1366x1024 viewport with no unexplained skeleton/error regressions.

## Work Breakdown
1. `CC-jtp0.7.2 - M7: Move Control Center code behind product boundary`: re-home Control Center runtime code under the product boundary without runtime behavior change.
2. `CC-jtp0.7.3 - M7: Split Control Center CI, images, and local dev by product path`: update path filters, image builds, product-scoped local dev, and iOS command ergonomics after the move.
3. `CC-jtp0.7.4 - M7: Declare Control Center product infra and private app route`: express Control Center through platform primitives and add the `app.cc` private route while keeping the legacy dashboard route live.
4. `CC-jtp0.7.5 - M7: Provision Control Center product CNPG and backup target`: create/adopt the product database and platform backup path without destructive replacement.
5. `CC-jtp0.7.6 - M7: Rehearse Control Center database restore and row-count validation`: prove restore and validation on scratch before production cutover.
6. `CC-jtp0.7.7 - M7: Cut Control Center production data to product CNPG`: execute write freeze, final snapshot, restore, validation, deploy, and rollback retention.
7. `CC-jtp0.7.8 - M7: Update Control Center iOS kiosk to app.cc private route`: ship the TestFlight kiosk URL update with Access headers and watchdog behavior preserved.
8. `CC-jtp0.7.9 - M7: Smoke Control Center workers and production integrations after cutover`: verify api, workers, media-worker, cron jobs, mounts, and integration health.
9. `CC-jtp0.7.10 - M7: Verify wall panel at true 1366x1024 on private route`: capture the real fixed-viewport UI and installed iPad verification.
10. `CC-jtp0.7.11 - M7: Update Control Center product docs and retire legacy route safely`: update runbooks/docs and record the legacy route retirement checklist.

## Dependencies
- Milestone depends on M1 Platform Foundation, M2 Data Safety Baseline, M3 Networking/TLS Contract, and M4 Product Folders/CI Split being sufficiently shipped. These are implementation prerequisites, not blockers for this scope ticket.
- `CC-jtp0.7.3` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.2`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.4` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.2`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.5` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.4`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.6` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.5`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.7` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.6` and `CC-jtp0.7.5`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.8` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.4`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.9` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.7`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.10` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.4`, `CC-jtp0.7.7`, `CC-jtp0.7.8`, and `CC-jtp0.7.9`.
- `CC-jtp0.7.11` depends on `CC-jtp0.7.3`, `CC-jtp0.7.4`, `CC-jtp0.7.7`, `CC-jtp0.7.8`, and `CC-jtp0.7.10`.

## TDD Structure
- `CC-jtp0.7.2`: red structural/package test or check that fails while Control Center runtime packages live only under `apps/*`, and passes when product-owned paths, package names, Docker contexts, and type-only API bridge imports are correct.
- `CC-jtp0.7.3`: red CI/path-filter or config test that proves `products/control-center/**` triggers the correct Control Center builds and unrelated product paths do not.
- `CC-jtp0.7.4`: red Pulumi/render tests for derived Control Center product namespace, labels, service names, secret names, `app.cc` private route, Access policy, and no `api.cc` route by default.
- `CC-jtp0.7.5`: red database primitive/render tests for CNPG resources, auth secret, rw service, and product backup path derived from product identity.
- `CC-jtp0.7.6`: operational red check is a missing/failing validation report until dumps, scratch restore, row counts, and semantic queries are recorded.
- `CC-jtp0.7.7`: operational red check is a pre-cutover validation script/report that fails until final snapshot paths, row-count expectations, semantic checks, rollback target, and approval are present.
- `CC-jtp0.7.8`: red Swift/config test that fails while default kiosk URL remains `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` and passes only with `app.cc` plus Access header injection/watchdog coverage intact.
- `CC-jtp0.7.9`: operational red checklist starts unknown/failing until production smoke evidence is collected.
- `CC-jtp0.7.10`: UI red check starts unknown/failing until true 1366x1024 screenshot, DOM skeleton scan, live data comparison, and installed iPad verification are recorded.
- `CC-jtp0.7.11`: docs/config red check is a grep/report showing old product path and dashboard references unresolved until updated or explicitly marked legacy.

## Verification
- Full code gates for implementation tickets: `bun run test`, `bun run typecheck`, `bunx biome check .`, `bun run knip`; in `.claude/worktrees/*`, use explicit paths for Biome due hidden-dir exclusion.
- Focused gates: package typechecks/tests for web/api/worker/media-worker, infra render tests, path-filter tests, kiosk Swift health tests, and map/backup script tests where touched.
- Pulumi: `pulumi preview --stack prod` before production route/database changes, verify `ccinfra:imageDigests.<svc>` remains the digest config namespace.
- Data: production pre-migration dump, scratch restore, migration run, row counts, semantic queries for device_state, integration_sync_status, weather latest reads, lamp_mode, media_source/media_item, and job queue.
- Cutover: write-freeze evidence, final dump paths, restore logs, row-count comparison, `/up`, `/health/climate`, worker/media-worker rollout status, logs, integration heartbeat SQL, backup CronJob completion, scratch restore from post-cutover backup.
- Private route: Cloudflare route/Access policy inspection, `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` browser smoke through Access, same-origin `/trpc` behavior.
- iOS: `apps/web/ios/scripts/test-kiosk-health.sh`, `bun run --filter @repo/web cap:sync`, successful `ios-build.yml`, TestFlight build metadata, installed iPad load.
- Wall panel: true 1366x1024 viewport asserted before screenshot, no unexplained skeleton/shimmer/error boundary after load settles, key tiles checked against live API.

## Rollback / Cutover
- Keep `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` live through route, iOS, and wall-panel verification. Do not retire it in early M7 tickets.
- Provision product route/database alongside existing resources where possible. Avoid destructive replacement of old CNPG/PVC/backup resources.
- For DB cutover: pause writers, take final snapshots, restore to product CNPG, validate, then deploy services against target. If validation or workers fail, redeploy services to old DB host/secret and scale down failing worker loops to avoid repeated actuator writes.
- Keep old database/PVC and all dumps untouched until product DB survives post-cutover backup and scratch restore validation.
- If Access blocks kiosk/browser, restore dashboard route as the primary URL and fix Access/token handling before retrying.
- If iOS route update fails in the field, keep legacy route serving the app or ship a follow-up TestFlight build pointing back to dashboard.
- Legacy route deletion is a separate, human-approved cleanup after M7 stability.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Private access: approve the `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` route and Cloudflare Access policy before production apply.
- Data cutover: approve production dump, write freeze, final snapshot, restore to product CNPG, and traffic cutover.
- iOS kiosk route: approve TestFlight upload that changes the production URL, and separately approve retiring the legacy dashboard route.
- Worker/production smoke: review production api/worker/media-worker/cron evidence before declaring the cutover stable.
- Wall-panel verification: review the true 1366x1024 screenshot and installed iPad behavior before closing the milestone.
- Legacy cleanup: approve any destructive removal of old routes, old DB/PVC, or compatibility wrappers.

## Docs To Update
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md`: product-owned Control Center paths, runtime shape, app.cc route, product DB/backup.
- `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` where load-bearing agent instructions change: product paths, commands, CI/deploy, iOS, and verification.
- `README.md`: monorepo/product layout and deploy path after Control Center is no longer repo-shaped.
- `docs/platform/*.html`: Control Center product status, data cutover result, route/private Access, local dev/iOS commands.
- `docs/deployment-design.md` and `docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md`: production route, product namespace/services, CNPG/backup path, rollback/runbook updates.
- iOS docs/workflow comments: hosted kiosk production URL, TestFlight trigger, Access token expectations.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Wall panel loads at true 1366x1024.
- [ ] No skeleton/error regressions.
- [ ] Workers run.
- [ ] Data cutover validated.
- [ ] iOS kiosk points at the new private route.

title: M6: Text Your Ex Product
description: Move Text Your Ex into the platform as a production product: products/text-your-ex, production app and API on homelab k3s, SQLite prototype to product Postgres, production starts empty while local dev can explicitly seed demo data, and bundled iOS TestFlight workflow targets the production API.
design:
## Goal
Make Text Your Ex a first-class world-wide-webb product in this repo: isolated under `products/text-your-ex`, deployed on homelab k3s with public web and API routes, backed by its own CNPG Postgres database and platform-managed backups, and shipped to TestFlight as a bundled Capacitor app that talks to `https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`.

## Scope
- Import the existing external app from `/Users/calum/code/github.com/0x63616c/text-your-ex` into the platform product layout without editing the source repo.
- Preserve the product behavior covered by its current Playwright E2E suite: auth, jars, slips, reports, activity, profile, invite/join, and the inert settle-up flow.
- Replace the SQLite prototype with product Postgres through the platform database primitive.
- Split production concerns cleanly: `app.tye.worldwidewebb.co` serves the bundled web app, `api.tye.worldwidewebb.co` serves the API required by web and iOS.
- Make production start empty by default. Demo data remains explicit for local dev and test only.
- Add product-aware CI/build/deploy path for Text Your Ex images without rebuilding Control Center for product-only changes.
- Add local Tilt resources and standard iOS commands for the product.
- Move/standardize the bundled iOS Fastlane/TestFlight workflow in this repo with `VITE_API_BASE=https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Add platform-managed backup and restore validation for the product database.

## Out Of Scope
- Real payments, payouts, committee voting, real SMS/OTP delivery, push notifications, contacts integration, or App Store public release.
- Extracting shared UI/backend packages out of Text Your Ex during the initial move. Keep it isolated first.
- Cloud runtime targets. Homelab k3s is the only implemented target in this milestone.
- Retiring the standalone source repo or deleting old TestFlight/App Store state.
- Reworking platform primitives that should already land in M1 to M4, except for small product-specific integration gaps discovered while wiring TYE.

## Current State
- This repo is still mostly Control Center-shaped: `apps/*`, shared `infra/src/services.ts`, `infra/src/cnpg.ts`, `infra/src/crons.ts`, one CI workflow building `control-center-*` images, and no `products/` tree or `packages/platform` present yet.
- Platform docs define the target shape: `products/text-your-ex`, namespace `text-your-ex`, short DNS code `tye`, public `app.tye.worldwidewebb.co`, public `api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`, per-product CNPG Postgres, mandatory NAS backups, product-local Tilt, and iOS commands.
- External Text Your Ex is a Bun workspace with `web`, `server`, and `e2e`; React/Vite frontend, Hono API, `bun:sqlite` storage at `server/data/tye.sqlite`, seeded demo data on first boot via `ensureSeed()`, and Playwright E2E coverage.
- External Dockerfile is a single image that builds web assets and serves static web plus `/api` from one Bun process on port `8787` with SQLite volume `/app/data`.
- External iOS is bundled Capacitor with `appId=co.worldwidewebb.textyourex`, `webDir=web/dist`; web client uses `VITE_API_BASE` for native builds and relative `/api` otherwise. Existing publishing docs mention a tag-based workflow, but no `.github/workflows` file is present in the source checkout.
- Source server has demo auth and pretend OTP. This is acceptable v1 behavior, but production must not auto-seed demo people/jars and must not leak demo data.

## Target State
- `products/text-your-ex` is a Bun workspace/product with web, API, E2E, Capacitor iOS, Fastlane, Docker build(s), and product docs living under that product boundary.
- The API uses Postgres, migrations run predictably, and IDs remain Stripe-style (`usr_*`, `jar_*`, etc.) unless a source behavior requires existing values in demo/test fixtures.
- Production API exposes `/api/health` or `/up` over `https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co` with valid public TLS. CORS permits the app route and bundled iOS use without making local dev painful.
- Production database starts empty. A seed command exists for local dev/test only and is never called by production boot.
- Platform infra declares Text Your Ex services through product/platform primitives, not copy-pasted Control Center service wiring. Namespace, labels, image names, route hostnames, secret mounts, database service name, and backup path derive from product identity where M1 to M4 primitives allow it.
- CI can build and deploy Text Your Ex product images independently. A Text Your Ex-only change does not rebuild or redeploy Control Center except when shared platform code changed.
- Product Tilt can run local Postgres, API, web, E2E, and iOS manual actions without starting unrelated products.
- TestFlight builds are bundled app mode and bake `VITE_API_BASE=https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co` only after the production API route and TLS pass human review.
- NAS backups land under `/volume1/Homelab/backups/world-wide-webb/text-your-ex/postgres/`, and restore validation proves the latest backup.

## Work Breakdown
1. `Import Text Your Ex into products/text-your-ex` copies the source app into the product boundary, wires workspaces, and preserves current gates without behavior changes.
2. `Port Text Your Ex storage from SQLite to Postgres` replaces `bun:sqlite` storage with Postgres-backed schema/migrations while preserving API behavior.
3. `Make Text Your Ex seed/demo data explicit and non-production` removes production auto-seed and keeps local/test seed intentional.
4. `Split Text Your Ex app and API runtime surfaces` creates production-ready app/API build modes, health checks, CORS, Dockerfiles, and env handling.
5. `Declare Text Your Ex production infra and routes` wires the product services, namespace, CNPG, public routes, TLS, image digests, and deploy path.
6. `Prove Text Your Ex backups and restore` adds/validates the platform backup job and scratch restore checks for the TYE product database.
7. `Add Text Your Ex local dev and E2E workflow` adds product Tilt resources, local Postgres, explicit seed/reset, and adapted E2E checks.
8. `Move and standardize Text Your Ex iOS TestFlight workflow` brings Capacitor/Fastlane/CI into this repo and points TestFlight at production API after approval.
9. `Finalize Text Your Ex production acceptance and docs` runs the production smoke checklist and updates platform/product/deploy docs.

## Dependencies
- Milestone-level prerequisites: M6 implementation depends on M1 Platform Foundation, M2 Data Safety Baseline, M3 Networking And TLS Contract, and M4 Product Folders And CI Split. These are linked on child issues rather than the scope ticket so planning stays unblocked.
- Import blocks all TYE code changes.
- Postgres blocks seed hygiene and production runtime wiring.
- Seed hygiene blocks production infra and TestFlight, because production must start empty before public surfaces or bundled clients point at it.
- Runtime app/API split blocks production infra and local/iOS integration.
- Production infra blocks backup validation and TestFlight production URL cutover.
- Backup validation and TestFlight both block final production acceptance.

## TDD Structure
- Import issue: first add a repo-local gate that expects the product workspace to typecheck/build/test from `products/text-your-ex`; it should fail before the import is wired.
- Postgres issue: first add storage contract tests for users, jars, memberships, slips, reports/evidence, sessions, OTPs, and activity against a disposable Postgres database; they should fail against SQLite-only storage.
- Seed issue: first add a production boot test/assertion proving an empty database remains empty after API startup; it should fail while `ensureSeed()` runs unconditionally.
- Runtime issue: first add health/CORS/build tests or smoke scripts for separate app and API modes; they should fail against the single-image, same-origin prototype.
- Infra issue: first add Pulumi/render tests or previews asserting `text-your-ex` namespace, services, public `app.tye` and `api.tye` routes, CNPG cluster, digest keys, and no Control Center rebuild dependency; they should fail before product infra exists.
- Backup issue: first add render/cron tests asserting the TYE backup path and database target; restore validation is the manual proof after deploy.
- Local dev/E2E issue: first adapt the existing Playwright suite to run against a local Postgres-backed product; it should fail before local DB/API/web wiring exists.
- iOS issue: first add CI/config checks asserting TestFlight builds use `VITE_API_BASE=https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`; they should fail before workflow env is wired.
- Final docs issue: existing gates plus production smoke checks protect behavior; no code behavior changes should be needed beyond docs/checklist updates.

## Verification
- Local/product gates: `bun install --frozen-lockfile`, product typecheck/build, adapted E2E suite, product API health check, and root gates where shared infra/CI changes are touched: `bun run test`, `bun run typecheck`, `bunx biome check .`, `bun run knip`.
- Database: run migrations against local Postgres and production CNPG; verify empty production DB at first boot; verify local seed creates demo data only when explicitly invoked.
- Production web: `curl -fsS https://app.tye.worldwidewebb.co` returns the app shell and browser smoke can reach the API.
- Production API: `curl -fsS https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co/api/health` returns ok over valid TLS.
- Persistence: create a real production jar/user flow, restart/redeploy API, and verify data remains.
- Backups: trigger or observe the TYE backup CronJob, confirm dated artifact in NAS path, restore into scratch Postgres, and compare row counts/key queries.
- CI/deploy: confirm Text Your Ex-only changes build TYE images and do not rebuild Control Center images unless shared platform code changed.
- iOS: TestFlight workflow logs show `VITE_API_BASE=https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`; installed TestFlight build can authenticate/create a jar against production API.

## Rollback / Cutover
- Keep the standalone Text Your Ex repo and current local SQLite prototype untouched until production is stable.
- Production starts empty, so database rollback is low-risk: scale down TYE app/API, leave CNPG PVC and dumps intact, and redeploy after fix. Do not delete target database or backup artifacts until restore validation passes.
- Route rollback: remove or disable `app.tye`/`api.tye` Cloudflare routes and scale replicas to 0; do not affect Control Center routes.
- API cutover is additive, no legacy public API to retire. If TLS or CORS fails, keep TestFlight pointed away from production until fixed.
- iOS rollback: keep previous TestFlight build available; do not expire or remove it when shipping the production-API build.
- If product infra causes unexpected cluster pressure on the 8GB homelab, scale noncritical TYE replicas to 0 first and keep Control Center prioritized.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Postgres migration/storage contract: human review before replacing SQLite with Postgres-backed production storage and before any production schema apply.
- Production routes/API: human review before creating or enabling public `app.tye.worldwidewebb.co` and `api.tye.worldwidewebb.co` routes.
- TestFlight production URL: human review before any TestFlight build bakes `VITE_API_BASE=https://api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Backup/restore: human review before declaring production data durable and before deleting any old SQLite/data artifacts.
- Public product launch: human review before treating the public routes as customer-facing rather than smoke-test-only.

## Docs To Update
- `docs/platform/PRODUCTS.html`, `DATA_AND_BACKUPS.html`, `NETWORKING.html`, `LOCAL_DEV_AND_IOS.html`, and `MIGRATION_PLAN.html` once implementation details are real.
- Product README under `products/text-your-ex` covering local dev, seed behavior, API base, production URLs, and E2E.
- Deployment docs/runbooks for Text Your Ex routes, database, backups, restore, and rollback.
- iOS publishing docs to match the actual workflow trigger and production API base.
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md` and `AGENTS.md` if product paths, commands, or platform conventions change.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Production web route works.
- [ ] API health works over valid TLS on api.tye.worldwidewebb.co.
- [ ] Database persists.
- [ ] Backups run.
- [ ] TestFlight targets the production API.
notes: Implementation dependency note: depends on M1/M2/M3/M4. Kept as notes, not blocker links, so the scope ticket remains ready for planning.

title: M5: Captive Portal Product
description: Split Captive Portal into its own product: product-owned backend/API/storage, move portal data out of Control Center Postgres with export/rehearsal/final snapshot/validation/rollback, and preserve UniFi guest onboarding, split DNS, cert-manager TLS, firewall/walled-garden rules, and Apple CNA behavior.
design:
## Goal
Captive Portal becomes a standalone product with product-owned frontend, API, storage, database backups, deploy/runtime configuration, and captive-network exposure. Real guest onboarding continues to work on the production guest network through the platform product hostname `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` with valid LAN-served TLS, while preserving UniFi authorization, split DNS, firewall/walled-garden rules, and Apple Captive Network Assistant behavior.

## Scope
- Move Captive Portal ownership out of Control Center and into the `captive-portal` product boundary created by earlier platform milestones.
- Create a product-owned backend/API boundary for the portal flow instead of serving it from the Control Center API.
- Move portal data from Control Center Postgres into Captive Portal Postgres with inventory, rehearsals, final snapshot, row-count validation, semantic validation, backup, restore validation, and rollback.
- Preserve the existing guest onboarding flow: email code, authorization status, UniFi authorize-guest, already-connected/session-expired behavior, and raw-IP Apple CNA compatibility where required.
- Move the LAN TLS/hostname target to `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`, with cert-manager DNS-01 certificate coverage and no Cloudflare tunnel route.
- Update UniFi split DNS, guest redirect, firewall, and walled-garden behavior deliberately, with human review before live mutations.
- Update docs and runbooks as implementation lands.

## Out Of Scope
- Generic LAN web exposure as a reusable platform class. Captive Portal remains a special captive-network exposure.
- Public Cloudflare exposure for Captive Portal.
- Control Center dashboard auth, Cloudflare Access default-deny, or iOS kiosk URL migration.
- Bandwidth/data caps, admin UI, or new guest-management product features.
- Dropping legacy Control Center portal tables before post-cutover backup and restore validation proves the new path.
- Cloud target support beyond homelab k3s.

## Current State
- Frontend lives in `apps/captive-portal` as a Vite/React app with Storybook, e2e tests, nginx config, Dockerfile, and a same-origin `/api/trpc` client.
- Portal procedures live in `apps/api/src/trpc/routers/portal.ts`; domain logic lives in `apps/api/src/services/portal-*`.
- Portal tables live in Control Center Postgres via `apps/api/src/db/schema.ts`: `portal_guest`, `portal_code`, `portal_attempt`, `portal_authorization`.
- `portal-data-purge` is a CronJob in `infra/src/crons.ts`, running the API image against Control Center Postgres.
- Production captive-portal workload is still declared with Control Center services in `infra/src/services.ts` and proxies portal tRPC to `api.control-center.svc.cluster.local:4201`.
- Current certificate and nginx server name target `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co`; cert-manager issues `captive-portal-tls` in `infra/src/certmanager.ts`.
- UniFi guest network state exists in `infra/unifi`: `www-guest`, VLAN 20, guest network name `guest`, firewall pinhole to `192.168.0.147:80,443`, and split DNS for `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Runbook findings are load-bearing: Apple CNA must keep a raw-IP HTTP path, gzip stays off, and the product must not be tunneled through Cloudflare.

## Target State
- Captive Portal is product-owned under the platform product boundary, with product-owned frontend, backend/API, runtime config, image/deploy path, and tests.
- Portal API exposes only the captive flow through same-origin/internal API. Guests cannot reach dashboard procedures, mixed tRPC batches, Home Assistant, or UniFi directly.
- Captive Portal has its own CNPG Postgres cluster, product secrets, nightly NAS backups, and restore validation.
- Portal data is migrated from Control Center Postgres to Captive Portal Postgres with stable IDs and validated active/expired authorization semantics.
- Production traffic uses the product API and product database. Old Control Center portal tables remain untouched until the rollback window closes.
- LAN TLS for `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` is valid from the guest network. No Cloudflare tunnel ingress exists for Captive Portal.
- UniFi redirects/pre-auth allowances/firewall/walled-garden behavior are updated or explicitly preserved to support `app.cp` plus the raw-IP CNA path.
- Docs identify `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` and Control Center API/Postgres coupling as legacy once cutover completes.

## Work Breakdown
1. `CC-jtp0.5.2 - Inventory captive portal ownership and cutover contract`: record current/target contract, rejected alternatives, and risky checkpoints.
2. `CC-jtp0.5.3 - Move captive portal frontend into product boundary`: re-home frontend app, Storybook, nginx, Dockerfile, tests, and CI path ownership without behavior change.
3. `CC-jtp0.5.4 - Extract portal API into captive-portal product backend`: create the product-owned API/service boundary and preserve the scoped guest API contract.
4. `CC-jtp0.5.5 - Provision captive-portal Postgres, backups, and restore validation`: declare product CNPG, backups, secrets, and restore validation.
5. `CC-jtp0.5.6 - Build portal data export, import, rehearsal, and validation tooling`: build and test safe data movement from Control Center to Captive Portal Postgres.
6. `CC-jtp0.5.7 - Cut captive portal runtime to product API and product database`: switch deployed runtime after rehearsed migration and final snapshot.
7. `CC-jtp0.5.8 - Cut LAN TLS and captive hostname to app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`: update certificate, nginx/server names, forwarding/service identity, and no-tunnel guarantees.
8. `CC-jtp0.5.9 - Codify UniFi redirect, split DNS, firewall, and walled garden for app.cp`: update/adopt live UniFi behavior with review and readback.
9. `CC-jtp0.5.10 - Run production guest onboarding cutover and final validation`: execute approved final snapshot, production switch, real-device validation, backup/restore check.
10. `CC-jtp0.5.11 - Remove legacy Control Center portal coupling and update docs`: clean up old coupling only after production validation and update runbooks/docs.

## Dependencies
- External milestone dependency: M5 implementation should start only after M1 platform primitives, M2 data safety baseline, M3 networking/TLS contract, and M4 product folders/CI split are usable. This remains a planning note, not a blocker link, so child tickets are visible.
- `CC-jtp0.5.3`, `CC-jtp0.5.4`, `CC-jtp0.5.5`, and `CC-jtp0.5.6` depend on `CC-jtp0.5.2`.
- `CC-jtp0.5.6` additionally depends on `CC-jtp0.5.5`.
- `CC-jtp0.5.7` depends on `CC-jtp0.5.3`, `CC-jtp0.5.4`, `CC-jtp0.5.5`, and `CC-jtp0.5.6`.
- `CC-jtp0.5.8` depends on `CC-jtp0.5.3` and `CC-jtp0.5.4`.
- `CC-jtp0.5.9` depends on `CC-jtp0.5.8`.
- `CC-jtp0.5.10` depends on `CC-jtp0.5.7`, `CC-jtp0.5.8`, and `CC-jtp0.5.9`.
- `CC-jtp0.5.11` depends on `CC-jtp0.5.10`.

## TDD Structure
- `CC-jtp0.5.2`: decision ticket, no code. Completion requires recorded inventory, target contract, rejected alternatives, and checkpoints.
- `CC-jtp0.5.3`: red-first package-boundary/import test proving the frontend no longer depends on the old app location or undeclared cross-product paths.
- `CC-jtp0.5.4`: red-first API boundary/scoped-proxy test proving portal procedures are product-owned and non-portal/mixed batches are rejected.
- `CC-jtp0.5.5`: red-first infra/unit test proving Captive Portal declares a product CNPG database and derived backup path.
- `CC-jtp0.5.6`: red-first fixture migration test for portal rows, IDs, timestamps, FKs, active/expired authorizations, newest code, and attempt lockout semantics.
- `CC-jtp0.5.7`: red-first integration/smoke test proving runtime reads/writes product Postgres rather than Control Center Postgres.
- `CC-jtp0.5.8`: red-first infra/nginx test for `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` certificate DNS names, nginx server name, and no Cloudflare tunnel route.
- `CC-jtp0.5.9`: red-first UniFi manifest/provider test or dry-run diff check for `app.cp` split DNS/guest access, with explicit human-console fallback if the provider cannot own a field safely.
- `CC-jtp0.5.10`: ops ticket, no code. Requires prerequisite gates green and production evidence.
- `CC-jtp0.5.11`: red-first boundary/dead-code/docs check proving legacy Control Center portal coupling is gone or explicitly quarantined.

## Verification
- Code gates for code-changing tickets: `bun run test`, `bun run typecheck`, `bunx biome check .`, plus `bun run knip` for cleanup.
- Captive Portal focused tests: frontend flow/effects/screen tests, e2e smoke/matrix tests, portal router/service tests, nginx scoped-proxy guard.
- Infra verification: Pulumi previews for product services, CNPG, backups, cert-manager, and UniFi; `kubectl get certificate`; `kubectl logs`; service/pod readiness.
- Data verification: custom-format and SQL gzip dumps, scratch restore/import, row counts, semantic queries for active/expired status, code lookup, attempts, and authorization rows.
- Network verification: LAN `curl`/`openssl` for `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`, guest DNS resolution through UniFi, no Cloudflare tunnel ingress, firewall/walled-garden readback.
- Production verification: real device on `www-guest`, Apple CNA behavior on the approved raw-IP captive path, full email/code/authorize flow, original URL redirect, internet access, and post-auth LAN restrictions.
- Backup verification: first post-cutover captive-portal backup exists, restores to scratch, and matches expected counts.

## Rollback / Cutover
- Before cutover, old Control Center portal tables remain source-of-truth and unchanged.
- Every data movement step must produce a rollback artifact: custom-format dump, plain SQL gzip, row-count report, and semantic validation report.
- Runtime cutover rollback: redeploy prior portal/API config pointing at Control Center API/Postgres and leave product DB read-only for investigation.
- LAN TLS/hostname rollback: restore old `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` certificate/server name/DNS/forwarding and leave `app.cp` unused.
- UniFi rollback: restore previous `guest_access`, DNS, firewall, and walled-garden settings from read-back baseline.
- Legacy cleanup rollback: do not drop old portal tables or retire old hostname until at least one successful post-cutover backup and restore validation cycle.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Before creating or mutating production Captive Portal CNPG resources.
- Before any production portal data export/import, write freeze, restore, or final snapshot.
- Before switching production runtime to the product API/database.
- Before LAN TLS/hostname cutover to `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Before UniFi redirect, split DNS, firewall, or walled-garden changes.
- Before the real production guest onboarding cutover on `www-guest`.
- Before destructive legacy cleanup, including dropping old portal tables, retiring old DNS, or removing old routes.

## Docs To Update
- `docs/platform/README.html`, `docs/platform/PRODUCTS.html`, `docs/platform/NETWORKING.html`, and `docs/platform/DATA_AND_BACKUPS.html` with actual M5 implementation details.
- `docs/captive-portal/PRD.md`, `docs/captive-portal/runbook.md`, and `docs/captive-portal/tls.md` for `app.cp`, product-owned API/storage, CNA/raw-IP behavior, UniFi settings, and rollback.
- `docs/deployment-design.md` and `docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md` for deploy, certificates, host forwarding, backup, and recovery changes.
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md` and `AGENTS.md` if paths, commands, or product ownership rules change.
- Any scripts/runbooks touched by LAN forwarding, UniFi, backup, restore, or migration tooling.
