title: M4: Product Folders And CI Split
description: Create product folders and split CI/deploy paths: products/control-center, products/captive-portal, products/text-your-ex, and products/amp; move product-owned code behind those boundaries; introduce product workflows for CI/deploy; optimize for product isolation enough to prevent unrelated rebuild/deploy churn.
design:
## Goal
Make product ownership visible in the repository and make CI/deploy product-aware enough that one product can change without rebuilding or deploying unrelated products. M4 is a structural isolation milestone, not a hostname, database, or production behavior cutover.

## Scope
- Create the `products/<name>` folder shape for `control-center`, `captive-portal`, `text-your-ex`, and `amp`.
- Move existing product-owned code behind product folders while preserving current runtime behavior, image names, k8s workload names, and public routes unless a later milestone explicitly changes them.
- Split CI/deploy path filters and workflows so Control Center, Captive Portal, Text Your Ex, and AMP have product lanes.
- Keep shared platform code in root `packages/`, `infra/`, and shared scripts until a later platform primitive extraction proves a better home.
- Update local Tilt/dev entrypoints so a product can be run without starting every product.
- Preserve the Control Center iOS hosted-kiosk shell behavior while moving its files.

## Out Of Scope
- Moving Control Center to new hostnames such as `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Moving or splitting Postgres data, CNPG clusters, backups, or schemas.
- Redesigning Captive Portal backend/API/storage. That belongs to M5.
- Importing the full Text Your Ex app or shipping its production deployment. That belongs to M6.
- Building AMP beyond a minimal product folder and CI/deploy lane placeholder. That belongs to M8.
- Renaming the repository, GHCR image namespace, Pulumi project, or production k8s namespace.

## Current State
- Root `package.json` workspaces are `apps/*`, `packages/*`, `infra`, `infra/unifi`, and `infra/cloudflare`.
- Existing apps are top-level under `apps/`: `web`, `api`, `worker`, `media-worker`, `captive-portal`, `drizzle`, and `map-provision`.
- `apps/web` owns the dashboard, Storybook host, and `apps/web/ios` Capacitor hosted kiosk shell.
- `apps/captive-portal` is a frontend product, but its API/data path still routes through Control Center API.
- Dockerfiles copy top-level `apps/*` manifests explicitly, so any move must update Docker build contexts and cache manifests.
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` is one workflow with per-image path filters and a single deploy job that pushes digest pins to Pulumi.
- `.github/workflows/ios-build.yml` watches `apps/web/ios/**`, `apps/web/capacitor.config.ts`, and `apps/web/package.json`.
- `Tiltfile` starts only the Control Center stack plus Storybook and does not have product-selectable entries.
- `infra/src/services.ts` deploys all current workloads as one Control Center namespace stack and reads image digest keys by service name.

## Target State
- Product code lives under:
  - `products/control-center/apps/{web,api,worker,media-worker,drizzle,map-provision}`
  - `products/captive-portal/apps/web`
  - `products/text-your-ex/` as an empty, non-deployed product shell ready for M6 import
  - `products/amp/` as an empty, non-deployed product shell ready for M8 build
- Root workspaces include product workspace globs and continue to include shared platform workspaces.
- Package names and import aliases either stay compatible or migrate through deliberate aliases so the move is mechanical, reviewed, and testable.
- Existing production behavior stays unchanged: same images, routes, k8s workload names, current Control Center iOS URL, and current Captive Portal LAN behavior.
- CI has product lanes and machine-checked path-filter behavior. A Text Your Ex-only change does not rebuild or deploy Control Center unless shared platform code changed.
- Local development has product entrypoints, with the existing `bun run dev` still running Control Center for backwards compatibility.

## Work Breakdown
1. `Add product folder workspace skeleton and manifest contract`: create product folders, workspace globs, and lightweight product metadata without moving runtime code.
2. `Move Captive Portal frontend into products/captive-portal`: relocate the frontend product and update build, Storybook, Docker, and CI references without changing guest behavior.
3. `Move Control Center runtime apps into products/control-center`: relocate web, API, workers, Drizzle wrapper, map provisioner, and iOS shell while keeping current images and runtime behavior.
4. `Seed Text Your Ex and AMP product shells`: add non-deployed product shells and package boundaries so CI can filter them without importing their real apps yet.
5. `Split product CI/deploy path filters`: create product-aware workflow/filter structure and regression tests for unrelated product changes.
6. `Split local dev Tilt entrypoints by product`: make Tilt product-selectable while preserving the current Control Center default.
7. `Update product-layout docs and agent instructions`: document the new layout, workflow rules, and implementation boundaries.
8. `Run M4 cutover verification and remove stale app paths`: after human review, remove compatibility leftovers and prove no old top-level product app paths remain.

## Dependencies
- M4 implementation depends on M1 product identity/package shape being stable. Do not start the skeleton/move work until that foundation is available.
- Captive Portal and Control Center moves depend on the skeleton/manifest contract.
- Text Your Ex and AMP shells depend on the skeleton/manifest contract.
- CI/deploy split depends on all product folders existing and on the moved products having stable paths.
- Local dev split depends on the product folder paths for moved products.
- Docs and final cleanup depend on the moves and CI split.

## TDD Structure
- For package and product folder changes, write or update a workspace shape test first that asserts expected product package paths and no missing workspace manifests.
- For Dockerfile path moves, run a red build or static check first against the old paths, then update paths until the target image build resolves.
- For CI filters, add a hermetic path-filter regression script first with cases for Text Your Ex-only, Captive Portal-only, Control Center-only, shared package, and infra-only changes.
- For Tilt changes, add a static smoke script or Tiltfile parsing check that fails before product selectors exist.
- For iOS path moves, run the existing kiosk health script and make `ios-build.yml` path tests fail before updating watched paths.

## Verification
- `bun install --frozen-lockfile`
- `bun run test`
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bunx biome check .`
- `bun run knip`
- Product-specific build checks for moved apps: web, API, worker, media-worker, captive portal, Storybook, Drizzle, and map provisioner.
- CI path-filter regression script proving Text Your Ex-only changes do not trigger Control Center image builds or deploy.
- `scripts/test-kiosk-health.sh` from the moved Control Center iOS path.
- Pulumi preview or dry-run review for CI/deploy path changes before merging to main.
- Human review of the final path-filter matrix and moved production Docker paths before any main push that can deploy.

## Rollback / Cutover
- Keep image names, Pulumi workload names, routes, and k8s namespace unchanged during M4 so rollback is a git revert of path/layout changes, not a production data restore.
- Move one product lane at a time. If a moved product fails CI, revert that product move and keep the skeleton/manifest seam if it is already green.
- Do not delete old top-level app paths until the moved path is built, tested, and reviewed. Final cleanup is its own child issue.
- If CI path filters over-trigger, revert workflow split first because it is deploy-path risk. The old single workflow remains the known-safe baseline.
- If iOS path updates fail, keep the old hosted kiosk URL and revert only workflow/path references. No app-store cutover is part of M4.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Review and approve the product folder layout before moving production code.
- Review and approve Captive Portal Docker/nginx path changes before merging because they can affect guest onboarding.
- Review and approve Control Center Docker, iOS, and infra path changes before merging because they can affect the wall panel and production deploy.
- Review and approve CI/deploy path-filter matrix before merging because a false negative can skip production deploys and a false positive can rebuild/deploy unrelated products.
- Review and approve stale-path deletion after all moved products pass gates.

## Docs To Update
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md`
- `AGENTS.md`
- `README.md`
- `docs/platform/README.html`
- `docs/platform/PRODUCTS.html`
- `docs/platform/MIGRATION_PLAN.html`
- `docs/platform/LOCAL_DEV_AND_IOS.html`
- `docs/deployment-design.md`
- `docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md`
- Any product README files created under `products/<name>`
acceptance_criteria: - [ ] A Text Your Ex-only change does not rebuild/deploy Control Center unless shared platform code changed.

title: M3: Networking And TLS Contract
description: Define networking and TLS contract: model all route exposure through platform primitives, preserve TLS/cert behavior during hostname cutover, implement private web through Cloudflare Access, treat Captive Portal as a special UniFi/captive exposure, and move old hostnames to app.<product> surfaces with explicit API hostnames only where declared.
design:
## Goal
M3 makes networking and TLS a product-platform contract instead of a set of hand-maintained hostnames. Products declare public, private, captive, or internal exposure, and the platform derives hostnames, Cloudflare routes, Access policy, cert-manager TLS, UniFi captive networking, and cutover verification. The dashboard, captive portal, private tooling routes, and nested-host TLS must all verify on the new model before legacy hosts are retired.

## Scope
- Define the typed exposure contract for `publicWeb()`, `privateWeb()`, `captivePortalWeb()`, and `internalService()`.
- Derive product service hostnames from product identity and service host intent, e.g. `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co`, `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co`, `app.tye.worldwidewebb.co`, and `api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Move Cloudflare tunnel/DNS declarations toward product exposures while explicitly preserving legacy hosts until reviewed cutover.
- Implement private web as Cloudflare Access: default-deny wildcard floor plus explicit allows/service-token paths where required.
- Preserve nested-host TLS for Cloudflare-proxied and LAN-served hosts. `*.worldwidewebb.co` is not enough for `app.<product>.worldwidewebb.co`.
- Treat Captive Portal as a special UniFi/captive exposure: split DNS, guest redirect, walled garden/firewall, host LAN forwarding, cert-manager DNS-01, and Apple CNA verification.
- Cut Control Center's dashboard and iOS kiosk from `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` to `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` only after route, Access, and TestFlight verification.
- Produce an explicit hostname retirement checklist and update operational docs/runbooks.

## Out Of Scope
- Product folder moves under `products/<name>` except where a minimum platform interface is needed to consume exposure declarations.
- Database extraction, data migration, backups, and restore drills, owned by the data/backups milestone.
- Captive Portal product/backend/database split, owned by the Captive Portal product milestone.
- Text Your Ex and AMP product implementation beyond ensuring their future public/private route shapes are representable.
- Generic LAN web exposure. Captive Portal remains the only LAN-like web primitive.
- Destructive cleanup of legacy routes before human-approved cutover.

## Current State
- `packages/platform` does not exist yet. Current infra uses low-level `WorkloadSpec` and `PortSpec.expose` in `infra/src/component.ts` with `cluster`/`lan`/`none`, not product-level exposure intent.
- `infra/cloudflare/src/routes.ts` is an adopt-only mirror of live Cloudflare tunnel and proxied CNAMEs: `dashboard`, `storybook`, `drizzle`, `hooks`, `portainer`, plus stray CNAME-only `hooks-test`. Captive Portal is intentionally absent.
- `infra/cloudflare/src/access.ts` adopts only deployed Access apps for `storybook` and `drizzle` with email OTP. The wildcard block floor and dashboard kiosk service-token access are planned but not deployed.
- `infra/src/certmanager.ts` installs cert-manager, a Cloudflare DNS-01 `ClusterIssuer`, and a `Certificate` for `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` into `captive-portal-tls`. It must keep public recursive DNS-01 self-check flags because split-horizon DNS breaks in-cluster SOA self-check.
- `infra/src/services.ts` deploys `captive-portal` as a LAN `LoadBalancer` on 80/443 and mounts `captive-portal-tls`. `cloudflared` runs in-cluster with two replicas.
- `infra/unifi` adopts live UniFi state and can create `www-guest`. Current adopted split DNS maps `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` to `192.168.0.147`; guest access redirect remains an explicit future change.
- Captive Portal nginx serves `server_name captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` and the entrypoint placeholder cert subject still uses the old host.
- `apps/web/capacitor.config.ts` points the iOS kiosk at `https://dashboard.worldwidewebb.co`; CF Access header injection is native in the iOS shell, not Capacitor server headers.

## Target State
- Product exposure intent is expressed once and reused by infra/cloudflare, cert-manager, UniFi/captive configuration, and workload/service wiring.
- `privateWeb()` creates a Cloudflare route plus Access policy. New private routes are born locked, with explicit allow/service-token policy and no accidental public exposure.
- `publicWeb()` creates only explicitly requested public web/API hostnames.
- `captivePortalWeb()` creates no Cloudflare tunnel route and instead drives cert-manager nested TLS, UniFi split DNS/redirect/firewall expectations, and host LAN-forward verification.
- `internalService()` creates cluster-only service discovery and no external DNS.
- `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` is the private dashboard host. The old `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` remains active until the iOS/TestFlight cutover proves the wall panel works.
- `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` is the captive portal host with valid LAN-served TLS and verified guest-network behavior.
- Legacy hosts are removed, retained, or redirected only through an explicit human-approved checklist.

## Work Breakdown
1. `CC-jtp0.3.2 - Define product exposure contract for networking/TLS`: create the typed exposure model and red-first tests for hostname derivation, API opt-in, and nested TLS requirements.
2. `CC-jtp0.3.3 - Derive Cloudflare tunnel and DNS routes from product exposures`: generate Cloudflare tunnel/CNAME state from exposure declarations while preserving legacy route behavior until cutover.
3. `CC-jtp0.3.4 - Implement private-web Cloudflare Access policy contract`: derive Access apps/policies from `privateWeb()`, including the wildcard block floor and service-token/email-OTP cases.
4. `CC-jtp0.3.5 - Add nested-host cert-manager TLS for captive portal`: issue and mount valid cert-manager TLS for `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` without breaking DNS-01 self-checks.
5. `CC-jtp0.3.6 - Cut captive portal network path to app.cp`: move UniFi split DNS, guest redirect, walled garden/firewall, and host LAN-forward verification to `app.cp` after TLS is ready.
6. `CC-jtp0.3.7 - Cut Control Center dashboard and iOS kiosk to app.cc`: route dashboard through `app.cc`, update Capacitor/iOS kiosk URL, and keep legacy dashboard alive until TestFlight/wall-panel verification.
7. `CC-jtp0.3.8 - Retire legacy hostnames through an explicit cutover checklist`: inventory and explicitly keep, redirect, or remove old Cloudflare/UniFi/iOS host dependencies.
8. `CC-jtp0.3.9 - Update networking/TLS docs and platform runbooks`: update platform and operations docs to match the implemented contract and rollback steps.

## Dependencies
- External dependency: M1 exposure primitive shape must exist or be created as the first slice of `CC-jtp0.3.2 - Define product exposure contract for networking/TLS`.
- `CC-jtp0.3.3 - Derive Cloudflare tunnel and DNS routes from product exposures` depends on `CC-jtp0.3.2 - Define product exposure contract for networking/TLS`.
- `CC-jtp0.3.4 - Implement private-web Cloudflare Access policy contract` depends on `CC-jtp0.3.2 - Define product exposure contract for networking/TLS`.
- `CC-jtp0.3.5 - Add nested-host cert-manager TLS for captive portal` depends on `CC-jtp0.3.2 - Define product exposure contract for networking/TLS`.
- `CC-jtp0.3.6 - Cut captive portal network path to app.cp` depends on `CC-jtp0.3.5 - Add nested-host cert-manager TLS for captive portal`.
- `CC-jtp0.3.7 - Cut Control Center dashboard and iOS kiosk to app.cc` depends on `CC-jtp0.3.3 - Derive Cloudflare tunnel and DNS routes from product exposures` and `CC-jtp0.3.4 - Implement private-web Cloudflare Access policy contract`.
- `CC-jtp0.3.8 - Retire legacy hostnames through an explicit cutover checklist` depends on route, Access, captive, and iOS cutover issues.
- `CC-jtp0.3.9 - Update networking/TLS docs and platform runbooks` depends on the final cutover checklist so docs reflect the implemented state.

## TDD Structure
- Exposure contract: first write unit tests for product slug to DNS label derivation, service host composition, no default bare product host, no default API hostname, and nested TLS requirement.
- Cloudflare routes: first update/add tests proving generated tunnel/CNAME output includes product hostnames, preserves legacy hosts until explicitly retired, and excludes `captivePortalWeb()`.
- Access: first add tests for `privateWeb()` -> Access app/policy, wildcard default-deny floor, kiosk service-token app, email-OTP tooling app, and no personal email literals.
- cert-manager/captive TLS: first add tests for `app.cp.worldwidewebb.co` dnsNames, TLS Secret naming/mounting, recursive nameserver flags, and nginx server_name/placeholder subject expectations.
- UniFi/captive cutover: first add UniFi tests for `app.cp` split DNS/redirect intent and a route test that prevents tunneling Captive Portal.
- iOS URL: first add a config/kiosk-health test asserting the default production URL is `https://app.cc.worldwidewebb.co` while local override still works.
- Checklist/docs: no code required unless automation is added; if automated, write parser/renderer tests first.

## Verification
- Unit/package tests: focused infra/cloudflare, infra/unifi, cert-manager/platform, captive-portal config, and iOS kiosk-health tests, followed by `bun run typecheck` for code-changing slices.
- Static guards: `bunx biome check` on changed paths, `bun run knip` when exports or package entrypoints change, gitleaks/no-personal-email for Access/secret-bearing changes.
- Pulumi previews: `infra/cloudflare`, `infra/unifi`, and root `infra` previews must show only reviewed diffs before any apply.
- Cloudflare checks: tunnel ingress/CNAMEs resolve, Access apps/policies match planned private/public exposure, default-deny behavior is verified without locking out required callers.
- cert-manager checks: `kubectl get certificate` Ready=True for nested host, Secret exists with expected keys, `openssl s_client` validates SNI for `app.cp` on the LAN path.
- UniFi checks: guest network DNS resolves `app.cp` to the Mini LAN path, redirect points at `app.cp`, and firewall/walled-garden allows only required portal 80/443 pre-auth path.
- Dashboard checks: browser/iPad loads `app.cc` at the true 1366x1024 wall-panel target with no skeleton/error regression.
- iOS checks: kiosk health tests pass, TestFlight build targets `app.cc`, and the physical wall panel has updated before legacy dashboard retirement.
- Captive checks: real `www-guest` network and iOS captive network assistant can complete portal flow over valid TLS.

## Rollback / Cutover
- Use additive-first cutovers: create new nested hosts, certificates, and Access policies before switching clients.
- Keep legacy `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` until the iOS/TestFlight wall panel is verified on `app.cc`.
- Keep legacy `captive-portal.worldwidewebb.co` certificate/server_name/UniFi DNS until `app.cp` verifies from real guest clients.
- For Cloudflare route or Access failures, restore legacy `desiredIngressRules`/`desiredCnames`/Access declarations and re-apply only after preview review.
- For cert-manager failure, revert Certificate dnsNames/Secret mount to the old captive host and keep DNS-01 recursive nameserver flags.
- For UniFi failure, revert split DNS/guest redirect/walled garden to the old captive host and verify guest clients recover.
- For iOS failure, ship or re-ship a TestFlight build pointing to `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co`; do not remove dashboard route until rollback path is proven.
- Retire old hostnames only from the checklist and only after smoke checks for all replacement paths pass.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Hostname cutover: approval before any production Cloudflare DNS/tunnel apply that moves or retires dashboard, captive, hooks, portainer, or tooling hostnames.
- Cloudflare Access: approval before wildcard block floor, dashboard private gate, kiosk service-token policy, or any policy that could lock out CI/hook callers.
- cert-manager nested TLS: approval before production Certificate/Secret changes for `app.cp` or any product wildcard.
- UniFi captive networking: approval before split DNS, guest redirect, walled garden/firewall, or host LAN-forward changes.
- iOS URL: approval before changing the production kiosk URL and before retiring `dashboard.worldwidewebb.co` after TestFlight rollout.
- Final legacy cleanup: approval before deleting/redirecting old hostnames or CNAME-only leftovers.

## Docs To Update
- `docs/platform/NETWORKING.html`: actual exposure contract, hostname matrix, nested TLS and cutover rules.
- `docs/platform/PLATFORM_PRIMITIVES.html`: implemented primitive names, app-team API, and non-configurable platform defaults.
- `docs/deployment-design.md`: operator-facing route/TLS/Access/UniFi/iOS recovery knobs.
- `docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md`: host launchd, cert-manager, Cloudflare, and UniFi networking details that remain load-bearing.
- Captive Portal runbook/docs: split DNS, guest redirect, cert verification, and LAN 443 forward.
- iOS docs/workflow notes: `app.cc` production URL, Access header behavior, and legacy dashboard retention rule.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Dashboard verifies on the new model.
- [ ] Captive portal verifies on the new model.
- [ ] Private routes verify on the new model.
- [ ] Nested-host TLS verifies on the new model.
notes: Implementation dependency note: depends on M1 exposure primitive shape. Kept as notes, not blocker links, so the scope ticket remains ready for planning.
close_reason: All M3 ACs complete: networking and TLS contract fulfilled. app--cp.worldwidewebb.co live on split-DNS (www-jtp0.3.6), guest WiFi portal flow confirmed physical by Calum.

title: M2: Data Safety Baseline
description: Establish data safety before product extraction: generalize the current pg-backup pattern into the platform backup primitive, keep current Control Center backups running, take a pre-migration custom-format Postgres dump, restore into scratch, validate row counts, and document restore commands and checks.
design:
## Goal
Establish the data safety baseline required before product extraction or data ownership changes. By the end of M2, platform Postgres backups are mandatory by construction, the current Control Center backup path is still running, a fresh pre-migration production dump exists, that dump has been restored into scratch, row counts have been validated, and restore commands are documented.

## Scope
- Generalize the current hardcoded `pg-backup` CronJob pattern into a platform backup primitive for product CNPG databases.
- Keep Control Center's existing production backups running while the primitive is introduced.
- Add or reuse tooling for custom-format dumps, plain SQL gzip dumps, scratch restores, and exact per-table row-count validation.
- Take a fresh production pre-migration snapshot before downstream product extraction touches data ownership.
- Restore that snapshot into a scratch database or scratch CNPG cluster and compare exact row counts.
- Document the restore and verification path in the platform data docs and operator deployment docs.

## Out Of Scope
- Moving product code into `products/<name>` folders.
- Cutting Control Center to a new product database.
- Moving Captive Portal tables out of the Control Center database.
- Changing retention from keep-forever.
- Replacing CNPG, adding cloud object storage, or introducing non-Postgres database standards.
- Deleting old dumps, old PVCs, or old pgdata rollback artifacts.

## Current State
- `infra/src/cnpg.ts` declares one single-instance CNPG `Cluster` named `control-center`, database `control_center`, owner `postgres`, local-path 5Gi PVC, and a bridged `cc-postgres-auth` basic-auth secret sourced by ESO from 1Password.
- `infra/src/crons.ts` declares a hardcoded `pg-backup` CronJob at `0 1 * * *`, using `ghcr.io/cloudnative-pg/postgresql:18`, `pg_dump | gzip`, and an NFS PV to `/volume1/Homelab` with subPath `backups/postgres`.
- `infra/src/component.ts` renders NFS PVs with mandatory `mountOptions: ["nfsvers=3", "nolock", "tcp"]`, which is required for the DS420+.
- `infra/test/crons.test.ts`, `infra/test/cronjob.test.ts`, `infra/test/render.test.ts`, and `infra/test/cnpg-certmanager.test.ts` already protect current CronJob, CNPG, and NFS behavior.
- `apps/api/src/db/schema.ts` is the production schema source, with key data tables including jobs, device state, weather history, media, and captive portal tables.
- `scripts/migrate-pg-to-cnpg.sh` has useful exact row-count and restore patterns, but it is Swarm-to-CNPG specific and should not be the only reusable restore proof for the platform migration.
- `docs/platform/DATA_AND_BACKUPS.html` states the platform target: per-product CNPG clusters, platform-managed backups to `/volume1/Homelab/backups/world-wide-webb/<product>/postgres/`, retained forever, with restore validation required.

## Target State
- Product database declarations derive a platform-owned backup CronJob without app-specific Kubernetes string soup.
- Control Center continues to produce daily backup artifacts throughout M2, with no gap in the current production backup path.
- The Control Center backup location is either already moved to the platform root or has a documented compatibility bridge and migration plan that prevents losing current `backups/postgres` artifacts.
- A fresh production custom-format dump and plain SQL gzip dump are captured outside the database PVC, with row-count snapshots stored alongside them.
- A scratch restore proves the dump is usable, migrations can run safely where appropriate, and exact per-table row counts match the source.
- Operators have documented commands for taking a dump, restoring to scratch, validating counts, checking the nightly CronJob, and rolling back safely.

## Work Breakdown
1. `Add product Postgres backup primitive` creates the reusable platform primitive and keeps the rendered Control Center backup safe.
2. `Prove Control Center backup continuity` verifies the live CronJob still exists, runs, and writes a dated artifact after the primitive lands.
3. `Add snapshot and row-count restore tooling` creates reusable commands for dumps, scratch restores, and exact row-count comparison.
4. `Take pre-migration production dumps` captures the human-approved production custom-format and SQL gzip snapshots plus source counts.
5. `Restore production dump into scratch and validate counts` proves the dump restores and row counts match before product extraction proceeds.
6. `Document restore and backup operations` updates operator and platform docs with the exact commands, checks, and rollback rules proven by the work.

## Dependencies
- M2 implementation depends on the M1 platform foundation database and backup primitive shape (`CC-jtp0.1 - M1: Platform Foundation`). Child issues are linked to make that ordering explicit.
- Backup continuity depends on the platform backup primitive because it verifies the production behavior after the generalized path exists.
- Production dumps depend on reusable snapshot tooling and backup continuity proof.
- Scratch restore depends on the production dump and snapshot tooling.
- Documentation depends on the primitive and the scratch restore proof.

## TDD Structure
- Backup primitive: start with failing infra unit tests that express the desired product backup declaration, platform NAS path, mandatory NFSv3 mount options, pg_dump image major parity, one-shot CronJob semantics, and unchanged Control Center schedule/name unless intentionally migrated.
- Snapshot and row-count tooling: start with hermetic tests for SQL generation, exact all-schema table selection, command planning/dry-run output, no secret-value logging, scratch target safeguards, and mismatch failure behavior.
- Production verification tasks: no code TDD, but each has a red-first manual check in its acceptance criteria, first show the current command or artifact expectation, then run the production check and capture evidence.
- Docs: protected by existing docs review plus gates, no implementation behavior is changed by docs alone.

## Verification
- `bun run --filter @repo/infra test` or the current infra test command that covers `infra/test/*.test.ts`.
- `bun run test`, `bun run typecheck`, `bunx biome check .`, and `bun run knip` before shipping code-changing children.
- `kubectl --context cc-homelab -n control-center get cronjob pg-backup` shows the CronJob present and not suspended.
- Manual backup trigger completes and writes a dated artifact visible on the NAS, without printing secrets.
- Production dump files exist, are non-empty, and include both custom-format and plain SQL gzip forms.
- Scratch restore completes, exact per-table source and scratch counts are shown side by side, and any mismatch fails loudly.
- Documentation includes the commands actually used and names the rollback artifacts to preserve.

## Rollback / Cutover
- If the backup primitive changes rendered production backup resources incorrectly, revert the infra change and re-apply the last known-good `pg-backup` declaration.
- Keep existing backup artifacts under the old path until the platform path has at least one successful backup and scratch restore proof.
- Production dumps are additive and must live outside PGDATA and outside the CNPG PVC, so rollback is to ignore the new dump and keep current production untouched.
- Scratch restores must target scratch only. If restore validation fails, stop product extraction, preserve source dumps and count files, and investigate before retrying.
- No data ownership cutover happens in M2. The output is a proven safety baseline for later milestones.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Human review before applying any production backup CronJob path/name/schedule change.
- Human review before taking the production pre-migration dump or final snapshot.
- Human review before restoring into any target other than scratch.
- Human review before deleting or moving old backup artifacts, old PVCs, old pgdata volumes, or snapshot files.
- Human review before marking the restore proof accepted if row-count output contains any mismatch or skipped table.

## Docs To Update
- `docs/platform/DATA_AND_BACKUPS.html` with the implemented primitive, backup paths, restore commands, and validation contract.
- `docs/deployment-design.md` with the operator-facing backup and restore runbook.
- `docs/k3s-migration/DESIGN.md` only where historical statements about current backups or paths have drifted from implementation reality.
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md` if new scripts or platform backup primitives become load-bearing entrypoints.
- `AGENTS.md` only if a new easy-to-miss invariant emerges, for example a required script for production snapshots.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Production Postgres has a fresh dump.
- [ ] Scratch restore has been completed.
- [ ] Row-count validation has passed.
- [ ] Restore commands are documented.
- [ ] Current backups are still running.
notes: Implementation dependency note: depends on M1 database/backup primitive shape. Kept as notes, not blocker links, so the scope ticket remains ready for planning.
close_reason: M2 data safety baseline complete: production dump captured, scratch restore and row-count validation passed, backups verified running, restore commands documented.

title: world-wide-webb platform migration
description: Umbrella epic for migrating this repository into the world-wide-webb product platform. End state: control-center, captive-portal, text-your-ex, and amp are first-class products under one platform with product namespaces, deploy, routing, TLS, secrets, Postgres, backups, local workflows, and iOS conventions provided by shared platform primitives.
design: North star: world-wide-webb is a product platform repo. App teams consume simple declarations. The platform owns shared primitives, naming, namespaces, TLS, secrets, backups, route mechanics, Kubernetes object wiring, local workflows, CI, deploy, and product database conventions. Products: control-center, captive-portal, text-your-ex, and amp. Homelab k3s is the only implemented target for this migration. Product layout target: products/<name>. Per-product CNPG Postgres clusters and platform-managed NAS backups are mandatory.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Milestone epics M1 through M9 exist under this umbrella.
- [ ] Each milestone has a scope ticket that plans executable implementation work before code starts.
- [ ] Platform migration docs and milestone epic designs remain the source of truth for scope, dependencies, verification, rollback/cutover, and human-review checkpoints.
- [ ] No migration implementation is done directly in this umbrella issue.

title: M1: Platform Foundation
description: Create the shared platform foundation: a packages/platform or equivalent shared platform package for product definitions and deploy primitives; product identity defined once; exposure classes; homelab target model; per-product CNPG database primitive with automatic backup; explicit secret catalog and service usage.
design:
## Goal
M1 creates the platform foundation as a typed, testable seam before product extraction starts. After this milestone, one existing product, Control Center, can be represented in the new platform model without changing production behavior, and later milestones can consume product identity, targets, exposure classes, secrets, CNPG databases, and backups without copying low-level infra wiring.

## Scope
- Add a `packages/platform` workspace, or equivalent shared platform package, for pure product/platform declarations.
- Define product identity once: slug, folder, namespace, DNS code, image namespace/name base, labels, Pulumi resource names, backup path roots, and service names.
- Define the target model with `homelab` as the only implemented runtime target while keeping target identity explicit.
- Define exposure declarations for `publicWeb`, `privateWeb`, `captivePortalWeb`, and `internalService` as intent-only primitives. M1 does not cut over any hostnames.
- Define a typed secret catalog and explicit service secret usage that can model the existing `SERVICE_SECRETS` inventory without broadening access.
- Define a per-product CNPG database primitive that can model the current Control Center cluster values without changing the running cluster.
- Define a platform backup primitive coupled to the database primitive, including the target NAS path contract, while preserving current production behavior until the data-safety milestone.
- Add a Control Center product manifest/fixture and equivalence tests proving it can represent the current infra model without behavior change.
- Update platform docs/runbooks to document the shipped foundation and what is intentionally deferred.

## Out Of Scope
- Moving product code into `products/<name>` folders.
- Renaming the repo to `world-wide-webb`.
- Changing production hostnames, Cloudflare routes, Cloudflare Access policies, UniFi DNS/portal settings, or iOS kiosk URLs.
- Moving or restoring any production data.
- Switching production deploy wiring to consume the new platform model unless guarded by exact equivalence tests and no-op output.
- Creating cloud runtime targets beyond a typed placeholder for future target support.
- Building AMP, importing Text Your Ex, or splitting Captive Portal data/API.

## Current State
- `infra/program.ts` is a single Control-Center-specific Pulumi program that installs ESO, CNPG, cert-manager, app workloads, and CronJobs in one namespace from `ccinfra` config.
- `infra/src/component.ts` has reusable but repo-specific `Workload`, `ScheduledJob`, and `ExternalService` components. Names, labels, secret names, and resource types are still app/service-level string inputs.
- `infra/src/services.ts` hardcodes Control Center workloads, GHCR image names, the `control-center-*` image namespace, HA ExternalName service, local-path PVCs, LAN captive portal service, replica knobs, and secret mount markers.
- `infra/src/secrets-map.ts` is a service-oriented map of 1Password `Item/field` strings for Control Center services, including Captive Portal needs mixed into the shared API.
- `infra/src/cnpg.ts` hardcodes a single `control-center` CNPG cluster, `control_center` database, `postgres` owner, `cc-postgres-auth`, storage/resources, and bridged 1Password password.
- `infra/src/crons.ts` hardcodes `portal-data-purge`, `map-extract`, and `pg-backup`; the backup currently writes to `backups/postgres` and is the prototype for platform-managed database backups.
- Workspace layout currently has `apps/*`, `packages/api`, `packages/logger`, `infra`, `infra/cloudflare`, and `infra/unifi`. There is no `packages/platform` yet.
- Existing infra tests already prove pure render behavior for workloads, crons, ESO, image digest pins, CNPG, and cert-manager. M1 should extend this test-first style rather than start with Pulumi apply.

## Target State
- `@repo/platform` exists as a leaf/platform package with pure functions and types for product identity, target context, exposure intent, secrets, database, backup, and service declarations.
- Product identity derives full product names outside networking, short DNS labels only for hostnames, stable Kubernetes namespaces, labels, Pulumi resource names, image names, and NAS backup paths.
- `homelab` is the only implemented target and captures target-owned constants such as domain, NAS backup root, and runtime constraints without leaking them into every product declaration.
- Exposure classes model intent and hostname derivation but do not mutate live Cloudflare/cert-manager/UniFi state in M1.
- Secret usage is catalog-backed and service-scoped, with tests proving current Control Center secret mounts are represented exactly and no service gains undeclared secrets.
- Database and backup declarations can render the current Control Center CNPG and backup shape in compatibility mode, while documenting the future `backups/world-wide-webb/<product>/postgres` target path for M2.
- A Control Center product manifest/fixture proves one existing product can be represented in the platform model with no behavior change by comparing generated declarations to current `serviceSpecs`, `SERVICE_SECRETS`, CNPG constants, and cron specs.
- Production remains on the current Pulumi wiring unless a child issue proves a no-op adapter with tests and requires no risky cutover.

## Work Breakdown
1. `Add @repo/platform product identity and naming model` - create the package and derive product folders, namespaces, DNS codes, labels, image names, Pulumi names, and backup roots from typed product slugs.
2. `Add homelab target model` - define the target contract and implement only the homelab target constants needed by product/platform primitives.
3. `Add exposure intent primitives` - model `publicWeb`, `privateWeb`, `captivePortalWeb`, and `internalService` as typed declarations with hostname derivation, without changing live routes.
4. `Add secret catalog and service usage model` - replace raw scattered secret references in the new model with catalog-backed service declarations and an equivalence test for current Control Center secrets.
5. `Add product CNPG database primitive` - express per-product CNPG database intent and render a Control Center-compatible CNPG declaration in pure tests.
6. `Add platform backup primitive` - express mandatory database backup intent and render a Control Center-compatible backup CronJob declaration, including the future NAS path contract but no production path cutover.
7. `Represent Control Center in the platform model` - add a Control Center product manifest/fixture that uses all primitives and proves equivalence with current service, secret, database, and backup declarations.
8. `Document M1 platform foundation contract` - update platform docs and agent-facing guidance for the actual package/API, no-op production stance, and follow-on milestones.

## Dependencies
- M1 blocks networking/TLS, data/backups, product folders, CI/deploy split, and product migration lanes.
- The homelab target model depends on the product identity/naming model.
- Exposure primitives depend on identity and target-derived domain rules.
- Secret catalog depends on the base platform package and identity model.
- Database primitive depends on identity and target model.
- Backup primitive depends on database primitive and target NAS/root path rules.
- Control Center representation depends on exposure, secrets, database, and backup primitives.
- Documentation depends on the implemented Control Center representation so docs match actual APIs.

## TDD Structure
- Product identity: first add failing unit tests in `packages/platform/test/identity.test.ts` for `control-center`, `captive-portal`, `text-your-ex`, and `amp` derived names.
- Homelab target: first add failing tests for target domain, NAS backup root, implemented-target guard, and rejection/unsupported status for non-homelab targets.
- Exposure: first add failing tests for hostnames and exposure metadata, including `app.cc.worldwidewebb.co`, `api.tye.worldwidewebb.co`, captive portal LAN intent, and no automatic external API host.
- Secrets: first add failing tests that render current `api`, `worker`, `media-worker`, `drizzle`, `cloudflared`, and `portal-data-purge` secret usage exactly from catalog declarations, with web/storybook/captive-portal absent when they declare no secrets.
- Database: first add failing tests that render Control Center CNPG database name, owner, auth secret, service name, size, and resource defaults exactly.
- Backup: first add failing tests that every declared product database gets backup intent, current Control Center compatibility output matches `pg-backup`, and future path derivation is deterministic.
- Control Center representation: first add a failing equivalence/golden test comparing the product manifest output to existing `serviceSpecs`, `SERVICE_SECRETS`, CNPG declarations, and cron specs.
- Docs-only child: protected by `bunx biome check` on changed docs/source where applicable plus review against this design.

## Verification
- Focused package tests: `bun run --filter @repo/platform test` and `bun run --filter @repo/platform typecheck` once the package exists.
- Infra regression tests: `bun run --filter @repo/infra test`.
- Repo gates for code changes: `bun run test`, `bun run typecheck`, `bunx biome check .`, `bun run knip`.
- No production apply is required for M1. If an implementation child touches `infra/program.ts`, verification must include `pulumi preview --stack prod` and evidence that the diff is empty or intentionally docs/metadata-only before any apply.
- Equivalence tests must prove one existing product, Control Center, can be represented without behavior change.
- Secret tests must assert values are never embedded, only catalog refs/keys and mount names.

## Rollback / Cutover
- M1 should be rollback-safe because it is foundation-only. Reverting the platform package and tests must leave current `infra/` deploy behavior intact.
- Do not run production hostname cutovers, database migrations, UniFi changes, or Cloudflare Access changes in M1.
- If an optional no-op adapter is wired into `infra/`, keep the old declarations available until equivalence tests and `pulumi preview --stack prod` prove no unexpected diff.
- Backup path changes are design-only in M1; actual backup path cutover belongs to the data-safety milestone with restore validation.
- Any production-impacting Pulumi apply must be paused for human review first, even if preview appears safe.

## Human Review Checkpoints
- Before any `pulumi up --stack prod` caused by M1 work.
- Before changing Cloudflare routes, Access policies, DNS, cert-manager certificates, UniFi settings, or captive portal LAN forwarding. These are not expected in M1.
- Before changing any CNPG cluster name, database name, auth secret, storage class/size, backup schedule, backup destination, or restore procedure.
- Before making `infra/program.ts` consume generated platform declarations instead of the current explicit declarations.
- Before declaring the platform API stable enough for parallel product lanes to start.

## Docs To Update
- `docs/platform/PLATFORM_PRIMITIVES.html` with the actual `@repo/platform` API and non-goals.
- `docs/platform/PRODUCTS.html` if product identity, DNS code derivation, or surface metadata differs from the seed docs.
- `docs/platform/DATA_AND_BACKUPS.html` for the database/backup primitive, compatibility behavior, and future backup path cutover boundary.
- `docs/platform/SECRETS.html` for the secret catalog shape and service usage contract.
- `docs/platform/NETWORKING.html` if exposure intent names or hostname derivation details change, without documenting unshipped cutovers as done.
- `CODEBASE_OVERVIEW.md` and `AGENTS.md` to point future agents at `packages/platform` once it exists.
acceptance_criteria: - [ ] One existing product can be represented in the new platform model without behavior change.
close_reason: M1 platform foundation implemented, verified by independent agent, and pushed to origin/main.

title: Fix web map-provision duplicate PVC pod volume rollout
description:
Deploying the web workload with the map-provision init container renders two pod volumes that both reference the maps PVC. In prod the new web pod stayed at PodReadyToStartContainers=False / Init:0/1 with only a scheduler event, blocking Pulumi deploy. Live mitigation on 2026-06-12 removed the init container from the web Deployment so web recovered; codify a real render fix so init and main share one pod volume for the same claim.

Acceptance criteria:
- renderWorkload emits one pod volume when an init container and main container mount the same PVC claim.
- Both init and main volumeMounts reference that shared pod volume name, preserving per-container readOnly settings.
- Web service spec keeps the map-provision init container.
- infra tests cover the shared-claim case.
- CI deploy completes without a web rollout deadlock.
notes: Fixed in fbdc2e1f: render now reuses an existing pod volume when an init container mounts the same PVC claim as the main container. Evidence: red focused test failed on duplicate maps pod volume, then passed after fix; bun run test infra/test/render.test.ts passed (26 tests); bunx biome check infra/src/render.ts infra/test/render.test.ts passed; bun run --filter @repo/infra typecheck passed. Note: bun run --filter @repo/infra test has an existing worktree-relative vitest project path issue and looks for infra/apps/api, so direct root vitest command was used for this file.
close_reason: Fixed in 5496e4ee and deployed by CI run 27421906590. Web map-provision init now shares the maps PVC pod volume with the main container; prod web and captive-portal rollouts are green.

Codify the distinction so controller desired state matches reality.

title: Captive portal SPA does not boot in Apple Captive Network Assistant (module script); CNA loads HTML then stops
description: Found in real-device testing after the UniFi external portal (CC-q002.15) + nginx upstream fix (CC-q002.25) landed. The end-to-end flow WORKS in a full browser (logs show /assets + fonts + POST /api/trpc/portal.sendCode from a Mozilla UA). But Apple Captive Network Assistant (the auto-pop captive sheet, UA CaptiveNetworkSupport...wispr) fetches only the SPA HTML at /guest/s/default/ and then makes zero further requests, no JS bundle, so the React app never mounts (blank/spin). Root cause: the Vite build entry is an ES `<script type=module>`; the CNA WebView does not execute it. Fix: ship a CNA-compatible build (Vite legacy plugin / nomodule fallback) or a minimal CNA-safe entry, and verify on a real device (pairs with CC-q002.17 cutover).
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] Diagnosed: nginx log shows CaptiveNetworkSupport (CNA) fetches ONLY /guest/s/default/ (the HTML) then no /assets/*.js, css, fonts, or /api, while a full browser fetches everything incl. portal.sendCode. The SPA entry is a Vite `<script type=module>` the CNA WebView will not execute.
- [x] Portal boots inside the iOS/macOS captive sheet: either a non-module/nomodule build (e.g. @vitejs/plugin-legacy so a classic-script fallback runs) OR a CNA-safe landing, verified by the CNA fetching the JS bundle + making the portal.status/sendCode calls
- [x] Real-device check: the Apple captive sheet renders the email/code screen (not blank/spin) and the flow completes
- [x] Gates green; committed fix(captive-portal/CC-xxx); merged + pushed + deployed; bd closed
notes: After Calum's iPhone still showed the captive sheet losing/stalling, live UniFi confirmed MAC 42:33:23:22:fd:2a is on SSID www-guest / network guest (VLAN 20), unauthorized, and has no guest auth session. Forget/kick/unauthorize did not resolve it because the phone immediately re-associated unauthorized. Captive nginx logs still showed CaptiveNetworkSupport fetching only /guest/s/default/ and no assets/API. Added a second fallback: index.html now has a plain classic ES5 loader script (script_cna_legacy_loader) that waits for modern boot, then loads Vite's legacy polyfill + legacy entry if window.__ccPortalBooted was not set. Build guard now asserts nomodule + legacy ids + the classic loader. Runbook documents UniFi network name guest and the CNA fallback.

comments.author: Calum
comments.text: RESOLVED + verified on a real MacBook: the SPA boots in the Apple captive sheet and completes the full flow (showed 'You are already connected' -> Apple success page). Fix = raw-IP HTTP captive landing (UniFi portal_use_hostname=false, redirect_https=false; live setting) + gzip off (committed b3725605, enforced by check-portal-nginx.sh). The CNA will NOT render the real-domain/HTTPS form (hostname-vs-IP, Apple ATS/captive heuristics); recovering the hostname is CC-q002.28 (low pri). Architecture + reset steps documented in docs/captive-portal/runbook.md.

title: Self-provision Tesla map tiles: runtime-resolved Protomaps build + web initContainer + refresh cron
description:
Prod Tesla tile broken after k8s migration: maps PVC empty because map-extract is a SUSPENDED manual CronJob nobody triggered, and its pinned source URL (build.protomaps.com/20260604.pmtiles) 404s anyway since Protomaps deletes daily planet builds after ~7 days. Two structural flaws: (1) provisioning depends on human memory at stack standup, (2) any hardcoded build date rots within a week.

Fix kills both: one small provisioner script/image (resolve latest build at runtime from builds.json, pmtiles extract bbox=-121.0,32.4,-114.0,35.9 maxzoom=15, atomic rename into the maps PVC) consumed three ways: web initContainer in if-missing mode (fresh stack self-heals, tiles become a structural precondition of nginx serving), unsuspended monthly CronJob in force-refresh mode (staleness), and kubectl create job --from=cronjob for ad-hoc runs. Plus nginx exact-location for /maps/ so a missing file fails loud (404) instead of silently serving index.html as 200.

Related: CC-gma (host pmtiles on homelab + expand extent) - the PVC+cronjob half landed with the k8s migration; this ticket makes it self-provisioning and rot-proof. Extent expansion to CA+AZ+NV stays in CC-gma (needs PVC resize: file ~1-3GB vs 2Gi claim).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Provisioner image (alpine + pmtiles CLI + sh script) added to repo and built by CI like other images; script resolves the NEWEST available planet build from build.protomaps.com at runtime (builds.json index), no hardcoded build date remains anywhere in infra/ (grep for 'build.protomaps.com/20' is empty)
- [ ] Script writes atomically (extract to tmp path on the maps PVC, rename into place) so nginx never serves a partial file; if-missing mode exits 0 instantly when socal.pmtiles already present
- [ ] Web Deployment gets an initContainer running the script in if-missing mode, mounting the maps PVC RW; a fresh stack standup serves the Tesla map with ZERO manual steps
- [ ] map-extract CronJob unsuspended, monthly schedule, same provisioner image in force-refresh mode (replaces the suspended manual recipe + stale 20260604 pin)
- [ ] nginx /maps/ location returns a real 404 for missing files instead of SPA index.html fallback
- [ ] infra tests cover: initContainer present on web, no date-pin regression, CronJob not suspended
- [ ] Prod verified: curl -H 'Range: bytes=0-99' https://dashboard.worldwidewebb.co/maps/socal.pmtiles returns 206 with binary (not HTML), and Tesla tile renders the basemap (screenshot @1366×1024)
--- auto-appended from DoD by type ---
- [ ] Provisioner demonstrated running (job/init logs showing resolve→extract→rename)
- [ ] Gates: bun run test + typecheck + bunx biome check green
- [ ] No fake data (check-fake-data guard green)
- [ ] Committed chore(infra/CC-xxx); worktree→main merged + pushed; bd closed

comments.text: Merged to main: 347394d8 (merge 66b43ad6). Prod verification pending CI deploy + initContainer first run.

title: Wire infra/unifi into CI gitops apply + re-audit Pulumi stacks for non-gitops gaps
description: GAP found while shipping CC-j934.3.2 (www-guest): the control-center-unifi Pulumi project is NOT wired into CI. CI only applies the cluster project (control-center-infra) and never references unifi, so UniFi/network changes can only be applied by a manual local `pulumi up --stack prod` in infra/unifi. That contradicts the gitops goal (everything applied by Pulumi via CI, not locally). The project SEPARATION is correct (cluster apply must never touch network state) but separate-project != manual-apply; a dedicated CI deploy job was never built. Feasibility confirmed: UCG-Fiber advertises 192.168.0.0/24 on the tailnet and the deploy runner already uses --accept-routes, so CI can reach https://192.168.0.1 without new subnet-router setup. Also re-audit cloudflare + any other stack for the same non-gitops gap. Interim: www-guest (CC-j934.3.2) is applied by a one-time local pulumi up until this lands.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] CI applies the control-center-unifi stack on push to main (own deploy job + path filter on infra/unifi/**), so UniFi changes ship via gitops, NOT a local pulumi up
- [ ] The job reaches the controller over the tailnet: UCG-Fiber already advertises 192.168.0.0/24 and the deploy runner joins with --accept-routes, so 192.168.0.1 is reachable (verify in-job before apply)
- [ ] UNIFI_API_URL + UNIFI_API_KEY sourced from op->GH secrets (no local env); zero-diff/adopt guard preserved (Boundary 1: additive only)
- [ ] Job stays ISOLATED from the cluster deploy (separate stack/job) so a cluster apply still never mutates network state (the reason the projects are split)
- [ ] Re-audit ALL Pulumi projects for the same gap: confirm control-center-infra (cluster) and control-center-cloudflare each have a gitops apply path; list any other manual-only applies and file follow-ups
- [ ] Docs updated (deployment-design.md / DESIGN.md) to state every stack applies via CI gitops + how the unifi job reaches the LAN
- [ ] Gates green, committed type(ci/CC-xxx), merged to main + pushed, bd closed
notes: Effort: MEDIUM/HIGHER than cloudflare. Core challenge = network: the UniFi controller is 192.168.0.1 (LAN-only), NOT reachable from a GitHub-hosted runner. Two paths: (A) homelab advertises 192.168.0.0/24 as a Tailscale SUBNET ROUTE so the CI runner (already joins the tailnet for k8s) can reach the controller - but that's standing infra + a security decision (CI gains LAN reach); (B, RECOMMENDED) run the unifi 'pulumi up' from a SELF-HOSTED runner ON homelab (already on the LAN) - no LAN exposure, natural home for LAN-touching steps (portal cutover too). Extra gotchas: the bridged @pulumiverse/unifi 0.2.0 provider needs 'pulumi up --refresh' (computed fields cause phantom diffs that wedge) so unattended applies are flakier; CI needs UNIFI_API_KEY + UNIFI_API_URL from 1Password. Only blocks the captive-portal cutover (itself human-gated), so lower urgency than www-cred.
close_reason: Code merged to main + cross-referenced commits; verified live (Calum) 2026-06-16. Live LAN/guest validation tracked in www-j934.11 + www-q002.17.

comments.author: Calum
comments.text: When wiring the unifi CI apply: it MUST run 'pulumi up --refresh' (not plain up). The bridged provider does not store controller-computed fields (e.g. multicastDns) in inputs, so without a refresh pulumi sees a phantom diff and the bridged networkconf Update 404s ('not found'), wedging the stack. With --refresh + declaring purpose:corporate and multicastDns:true (CC-j934.3.2), it converges to zero diff.

title: Rate-limit captive portal OTP sends: global sender-boundary cap (anti-quota-exhaustion)
description: www-guest is going OPEN (CC-j934.3.2) so anyone in range can hit sendCode (the OTP email route; portal SPA -> /api/trpc -> api tRPC portal router -> portalService.sendCode -> Resend adapter). Resend free tier is 3,000/month + 100/day and Resend ENFORCES it, so the threat on free tier is QUOTA EXHAUSTION (real guests locked out), not a runaway bill. Primary defence = a global cap at the sender boundary set under Resend free limits; this is the deterministic guarantee that survives MAC/email rotation. Per-IP/per-email = secondary. Storage = Postgres (no Redis; homelab is RAM-constrained), same DB-as-truth + injectable-clock pattern as portal_attempt. Existing RateLimited.tsx screen handles the device-lockout UX.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] PRIMARY: a GLOBAL email cap enforced at the EmailSender boundary (wrap the sender, not just sendCode) so every email path is bounded; atomic check+increment in Postgres before any Resend call
- [ ] Caps tuned UNDER Resend free limits: global daily cap < 100/day (e.g. 50/day) and global monthly cap < 3,000/month; config-driven, no hardcoded magic
- [ ] When the global cap is hit, sendCode hard-rejects with a typed PortalError (NOT a generic Error) and logs it; real guests see a clear message (see linked bug)
- [ ] SECONDARY: per-source (IP where it propagates through the proxy, else MAC) cap so one actor cannot eat the whole global budget; existing 30s per-email cooldown retained
- [ ] Resend dashboard backstop documented: account monthly cap + budget alert as defence-in-depth outside the app
- [ ] Test: exceeding the global cap returns a typed reject (red before / green after); under-cap traffic passes
- [ ] Gates green (test+typecheck+biome), no fake data, committed type(api/CC-xxx), worktree->main merged + pushed, bd closed
close_reason: Obsolete after www-p9hx: captive portal email-OTP is the wrong primitive inside Apple CNA, so OTP sender-boundary rate limiting no longer matches the chosen direction.

title: bosun cronJob named volumes not stack-namespaced -> job mounts wrong volume
description: The cron-job dispatch path (scheduler.ts mountFlag) used the raw volume source name, but swarm stack deploy namespaces volumes <stack>_<name>. So the portal-cert-renew acme job wrote the real LE cert into an orphan 'portal-certs' volume while the captive-portal service read 'control-center_portal-certs' -> portal stuck on its self-signed placeholder. Surfaced finishing CC-q002.22 (LAN-edge HTTPS). Affects every cronJob() with a named volume.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] mountFlag() prefixes a NAMED volume source with <stack>_ so a cronJob mounts the same volume as the stack-deployed service that shares it
- [ ] bind-mount (path) sources are NOT namespaced
- [ ] regression test: portal-cert-renew job mounts control-center_portal-certs (not orphan portal-certs)
- [ ] portal-cert-renew acme cert lands in the volume the captive-portal service reads; portal serves the real LE cert
- [ ] gates green; committed fix(bosun/CC-xxx); merged + pushed

title: bosun-agent rate-limits 1Password on deploy churn (no op-read cache, re-reads all secrets every bosun up)
description: Found 2026-06-10 (CC-q002). Every CI deploy makes the bosun-agent run 'bosun up', which resolves EVERY fromOp() secret in deploy.config.ts with a fresh 'op read' (~15+ CLI calls, one per secret), no caching in the agent container (unlike Calum's personal op shim). Heavy deploy churn (many deploys/hour across sessions) rate-limited the 1P service account ('Too many requests. Your client has been rate-limited'), after which EVERY bosun up aborts at secret resolution -> 'fresh-image deploy one-shot exited 1' -> NO image rolls, a silent prod-deploy outage. Worse: CI's deploy job reports success because the webhook POST returned 2xx; only the agent's internal one-shot exits 1, so the failure is invisible without reading agent logs on the box. See memory bosun-agent-op-rate-limit.
acceptance_criteria:
Pick + implement ONE durable fix so a deploy can never be silently blocked by 1Password rate-limiting:
- [ ] OPTION A (sync, preferred): resolve Homelab secrets into GitHub Actions secrets (a 1Password->GH-secret sync, e.g. 1P GitHub integration or a scheduled job), and have CI pass the needed values to the deploy webhook payload, so the bosun-agent does NOT op-read every secret on every 'bosun up' (op hit only on rotation, not per deploy)
- [ ] OPTION B (cache, if A not viable): the agent caches/batches op reads within a single 'bosun up' (one op session, not N cold CLI reads) and reuses resolved docker secrets across deploys when unchanged; only re-read on rotation. Must stay safe (no secret values written to disk/logs unencrypted)
- [ ] EITHER WAY: a transient 1P 429 backs off + retries instead of aborting the whole stack; and the CI deploy job surfaces an agent-side 'bosun up' failure (today it reports success on webhook-accept while the one-shot exits 1 - a silent prod-deploy outage)
- [ ] Test coverage for the chosen path (cache/backoff or the CI secret-passing)
- [ ] Gates green (test + typecheck + bunx biome check); no fake data
- [ ] Committed fix(bosun/CC-nbjv) [or ci/ if the fix is CI-side]; worktree->main merged + pushed; bd closed
close_reason: Obsolete: bosun-agent/deploy.config.ts path removed by the Pulumi+k3s migration; deploy no longer runs bosun up or op-read secret resolution per deploy.

comments.author: Calum
comments.text: Calum (2026-06-10): we need to fix that op can rate-limit and cause issues. Caching values is fine if safe; but a better approach may be 1Password creating/syncing the value into a GitHub secret, then CI passes the gh secret (so the box isn't op-reading on every deploy). Captured as Option A above.

title: Climate tile: ambient caret off-card when temp exceeds 67-77 band
description: The climate slider scale is fixed 67-77 (MIN/MAX). When ambient temp is outside the band (e.g. 78), the 'Now' caret is positioned past 100% and falls off the card. Make the VISUAL scale dynamic, driven by ambient only: when ambient >= MAX-1, extend max to round(ambient)+2; when ambient <= MIN+1, extend min to round(ambient)-2; otherwise keep the default 67/77 band. Settable setpoint values stay clamped to [MIN,MAX].

title: Portal proxy boundary: mixed tRPC batches can smuggle dashboard procedures
description: Area: api. Found by CC-q002.14 validation: nginx matches the URL prefix ^/api/trpc/portal\. so a crafted batch /api/trpc/portal.x,lights.y matches and proxies WHOLE batch; the api would execute lights.y. The guest-VLAN boundary must not rest solely on a prefix match. Fix BOTH layers: (1) nginx regex only matches batches where EVERY comma-separated segment starts with portal. ; (2) api-side guard: proxy sets a marker header, api middleware rejects any non-portal procedure on requests carrying it (defense-in-depth against future conf regressions).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Regression test FIRST (red before fix): a mixed batch portal.status,controls-or-lights-proc through the portal proxy path is rejected, pure portal batches still work
- [ ] nginx location only matches all-portal batches (single + multi-segment), verified by the conf test or a container curl matrix
- [ ] api middleware rejects non-portal procedures when the portal-proxy marker header is present; header set in _portal_locations.conf; direct dashboard-origin calls unaffected
- [ ] Gates: bun run test + typecheck + bunx biome check green
- [ ] Committed fix(api/CC-xxx); merged + pushed per run-in-flight rule; bd closed

title: Wire portal flow effects to the tRPC client (the .7 to .9 seam)
description: Area: captive-portal. PRD: docs/captive-portal/PRD.md. Gap found by E2E prep + .7 validation: App.tsx runEffects() is a stub, no tRPC client exists in apps/captive-portal, so reducer effects (sendCode/verifyCode/checkPassword/resetAttempts) never reach the backend and server events (CODE_SENT/VERIFY_OK/...) are never dispatched. The flow stalls at 'sending' in a real browser. This ticket closes the seam between .7 (state machine, closed) and .9 (portal router, closed).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] tRPC client in apps/captive-portal with base /api/trpc (infra's nginx contract), MAC threaded into every call

title: Pre-commit guard: block personal email (hash-based) from public repo
description: Mirror the no-home-address guard for Calum's personal email. The repo is public (CC-4ma); the iCloud login email must never land in tracked content. base64 is reversible so it can't be used to hide an email (unlike the home-address building-fragment trick). Use a ONE-WAY SHA-256 of the email stored in the script; the guard extracts email-shaped tokens from staged files, hashes each (lowercased), and blocks on a match. Cleartext email never appears in the repo, and the stored hash is irreversible. Wire into lefthook pre-commit alongside the other blocking guards.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] scripts/check-no-personal-email.sh exists, contains NO cleartext email, only the SHA-256 digest
- [ ] Guard blocks (exit 1) a staged file containing [redacted-personal-email]
- [ ] Guard passes (exit 0) on noreply addresses and clean files

- [ ] Hermetic test scripts/test-check-no-personal-email.sh covers pass+block, exits 0
- [ ] Wired into lefthook.yml pre-commit stage

comments.author: Calum
comments.text:
Read-only live probe findings (CC-q002.10), controller https://192.168.0.1 with X-API-KEY (op://Homelab/UniFi/local_api_key):

- Controller: UniFi Network 10.4.57 (build atag_10.4.57_34628) on UCG-Fiber (Cloud Gateway Fiber). TZ America/Los_Angeles. UniFi OS.
- Auth + path: X-API-KEY header works against the legacy v1 API under /proxy/network/api/s/<site>/... (same path the existing UnifiClient.legacyRequest already uses, site='default'). Self-signed cert -> rejectUnauthorized:false (already handled).
- stat/sysinfo: 200, returns version/build (used above to confirm version).
- stat/guest: 200, {meta:{rc:'ok'}, data:[]} right now (no active guest authorizations yet). This is the ACTIVE-AUTHORIZATION lookup endpoint. Each row (when present) carries mac, _id, start, end (epoch seconds), site_id, etc. We will use it to cross-check the controller still holds an authorization for a mac (the DB row is source of truth per team-lead ruling; controller is reconciled/healed).
- cmd/stamgr: GET -> 404 (endpoint is POST-only, as expected). authorize-guest is POST /proxy/network/api/s/<site>/cmd/stamgr with JSON {cmd:'authorize-guest', mac:'<lower:colon>', minutes:43200}. I did NOT POST it live (write endpoint - tests only, against a mock).
- /proxy/network/status (UniFi-OS root, no /api): 401 with X-API-KEY (that path wants the OS session cookie, not the network API key) - irrelevant, we use the /api/s/<site> v1 surface throughout.

Plan: extend UnifiClient with authorizeGuest(mac, minutes=43200) [POST cmd/stamgr] + listActiveGuestAuthorizations()/findActiveAuthorization(mac) [GET stat/guest]. Introduce a UnifiGuestClient interface so the portal service depends on the interface (mockable). Tests assert cmd+mac+minutes=43200 + correct path, and that zero real network calls happen when the mock is used. Unreachable -> UnifiError (services throw).

title: Climate policy: adopt external thermostat changes instead of enforcing over them
description: Area: api. Climate control is currently ENFORCE (CC-unxz.2): a setpoint/mode change made on the physical ecobee or ecobee app is reverted by the climate-enforcer within ~200ms (verified in HA history 2026-06-10: 04:03 75→72 in 190ms; 04:40 70→72 twice). The thermostat has a physical interface just like the Shelly wall switches, which are policy 'adopt' - external drift should be absorbed as new intent, last-writer-wins. Change decideClimateEnforcement: dashboard taps still actuate via the 10s desiredUntilUtc command window (push); OUTSIDE that window, drift on commandable fields is absorbed (desired := reported) instead of pushed. Mirrors the lights' adopt policy.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] decideClimateEnforcement returns an adopt decision (desired := reported) for drift outside the desiredUntilUtc command window
- [ ] Inside the command window a fresh app-commanded desired is still pushed to HA (dashboard taps actuate unchanged)

title: Fix frozen climate ambient/action: desired must never carry reported-only fields
description: Area: api. The climate tile's ambient temp is frozen at the seed-time value (prod: desired.ambient=71 since seed, reported.ambient live). Cause: climate-enforcer seeds desiredState as a wholesale copy of reported INCLUDING reported-only ambient/action (climate-enforcer-service.ts seed + first-insert); writeClimateDesired bases each new desired on the previous one so they persist forever; mergeDeviceState's {...reported,...desired} overlay then shadows the live reported ambient/action. Fix at the right layer: desired only ever carries commandable fields (mode/target/targetLow/targetHigh/fanMode) - strip ambient/action at seed and on every desired write - and/or climate merge always sources ambient/action from reported. Existing prod row must self-heal (enforcer sanitizes stale desired on cycle).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] desiredState for the climate row never contains ambient/action: stripped at enforcer seed (both seed paths) and in writeClimateDesired
- [ ] mergeDeviceState returns live reported ambient/action for climate even when desired is stale
- [ ] Existing prod row self-heals: a desired containing ambient/action is sanitized by the enforcer cycle without a manual migration
- [ ] Regression test red-before/green-after: stale desired.ambient + fresh reported.ambient → getClimate returns the reported ambient
- [ ] Gates: bun run test + typecheck + bunx biome check green
- [ ] No fake data (check-fake-data guard green)

title: Backend-only Sonos max-volume cap at 90 (hidden from the frontend)
description: Sonos exposes no device-level volume limit over UPnP (verified live: RenderingControl/DeviceProperties SCPDs have no limit action) and HA has no entity/service for it either. Enforce the cap in our own enforcer instead: desired stays raw in device_state, the 1s sonos-volume-enforcer actuates min(desired,90) and converges against the clamp; >90 external changes are pushed back. Area: media.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] SPEAKER_MAX_VOLUME=90 cap lives in sonos-volume-enforcer-service; the enforcer pushes min(desired.volume, 90) to the speaker
- [ ] Convergence is judged against the CLAMPED desired (desired 100 + reported 90 = converged noop), so a desired above the cap never causes push loops
- [ ] External drift ABOVE the cap is pushed back down to 90 within one cycle (cap overrides adopt); external drift at/below 90 still adopts as before
- [ ] setSpeakerDesiredVolume stores the RAW requested volume (no clamp) and media.soundSystem keeps returning raw desired, so the frontend shows the user's value and never reveals the cap (no web changes)
- [ ] Decision matrix unit tests cover: hidden-cap noop, clamped push inside window, cap-overrides-adopt above 90, normal adopt at/below 90
- [ ] Gates: bun run test + typecheck + bunx biome check green
- [ ] No fake data (check-fake-data guard green)

comments.author: Calum
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED (2026-06-12, CC session): bd dolt push hung/failed because the GLOBAL git core.hooksPath (dotcalum dispatcher -> lefthook) leaked into dolt's INTERNAL git push for refs/dolt/data. dolt stages chunk files in a bare/worktree-less repo; its git push fires the global pre-push hook; lefthook's startup probe 'git rev-parse --path-format=absolute --show-toplevel ...' errors 'this operation must be run in a work tree' (exit 128) in a bare repo, which dolt surfaces as 'unknown push error; addTableFiles, updateManifestAddFiles ... exit status 128'. Slow first (dolt git+ssh round-trips, upstream dolt#10537) then SIGKILL when the hung push is killed = the 'Uploading...: signal: killed' symptom. Proven: lefthook's probe is byte-for-byte the command in dolt-server.log; reproduced exit 128 in a fresh 'git init --bare'. SECONDARY: lefthook had also clobbered the global dispatcher dir (wrote its own scripts over the _dispatch symlinks, machine-wide). FIX (dotcalum): (1) _dispatch bare-repo guard exits 0 when not in a work tree; (2) reconcile-hooks.sh heal_global_dir restores clobbered symlinks; (3) lefthook shell shim forces 'lefthook install' into the repo's own .git/hooks. All three covered by hermetic tests.

title: Make Sonos volume desired-state (DB-authoritative) with a 1s enforcer worker

Today SoundSystemTile writes straight to the speaker over UPnP (sonos-write-service) with no DB state; the 10s soundSystem poll can snap the fader back. Make device_state the source of truth for speaker volume: mutation accepts instantly (writes desired), a 1s worker reconciles desired→speaker via UPnP (RenderingControl SetVolume/GetVolume), external changes are adopted outside the command window. Area: media. (Recreated: original row was lost to a dolt pull race before sync.)

- [ ] sonosSetVolume writes desiredState + desiredUntilUtc command window to the DB and returns immediately (no direct UPnP call in the mutation path)
- [ ] New worker 'sonos-volume-enforcer' registered in apps/worker at intervalMs 1000, runOnStart: seeds desired from reported when null, pushes desired to the speaker on drift inside the command window or per policy, adopts external drift (Sonos app changes) outside the window
- [ ] media.soundSystem returns desired-authoritative volume (desired overlays live reported) so the fader never snaps back on the 10s poll
- [ ] Pure decision function unit-tested (seed / push / adopt / unreachable matrix), modeled on light-enforcer-service tests

title: Kiosk auto-refresh: reload dashboard when a new build is deployed
description: The iPad wall panel (Capacitor WKWebView kiosk; idle timer disabled, never reloads) keeps serving whatever bundle it first loaded. No client-side mechanism picks up OTA web deploys, so a newly-deployed SHA never appears until manual reload. Add a build-stamped version check: at build, emit a fetchable version stamp carrying the deployed SHA (SAME source as BUILD_HASH, baked via vite define); the web app polls it cache-busted every 10s (VERSION_POLL_MS=10_000) and also on visibilitychange->visible and window 'online', compares to the running BUILD_HASH, and calls an injected reload (default location.reload()) once on mismatch. Web-app side only (OTA, no App Store rebuild) per the documented OTA model - NOT a Swift reload timer.
acceptance_criteria:
- Build emits a fetchable version stamp (e.g. dist/version.json) containing the build SHA, matching the BUILD_HASH baked into the running bundle (single source).
- Web app polls it on an interval governed by a single named constant (e.g. VERSION_POLL_MS), cache-busted (cache: no-store), and ALSO re-checks on visibilitychange->visible and window 'online'.
- On fetched-SHA != running BUILD_HASH, app calls location.reload() exactly once; guarded against reload loops.
- SHA match => no reload. Fetch/network errors are swallowed (kiosk keeps running, retries next tick).
- Unit test (fake timers + mocked fetch): reload fires on mismatch, not on match, guarded against loops.
- Verified in a real headless browser (agent-browser): serving version.json then changing its SHA triggers exactly one reload to the new build.

title: Dev lifecycle system: new/starting/finish skills + ticket-standards + ship v2
design: Redesign how we work in this repo into a bulletproof ticket lifecycle: open -> /starting-ticket (claim+worktree+TDD) -> in_progress -> /finish-ticket (gates+commit+merge-to-main+push+close) -> closed. A single source of truth (docs/ticket-standards.md) defines a ticket taxonomy (feature/bug/refactor/chore/spike/epic), a base Definition of Done + per-type adders, a Definition of Ready, a priority rubric, and the enforcement matrix. Three skills make the happy path standardized; lefthook/guards make skipping mechanically impossible. ship v2 reuses the same taxonomy/DoD and worktree-per-feature isolation. No PRs ever; worktrees merge to main locally. Dogfooded: this epic is built through the lifecycle it defines.
